analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Section 5.8 (p.20) covers this directly.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
Section 1.5-1.6 (Page 3-4): The plan is candid about the immediate challenges to deliverability. It states it is "not yet compliant against the performance targets" and requires significant extra funding (£29m in 26/27) and workforce to bridge the gap. Its financial plan to reach balance by year 3 (28/29) is a critical enabler.
The plan includes specific productivity and efficiency initiatives (e.g., "optimised skill mix," "reduction of unwarranted variation") which are essential for delivering the 10 Year Plan's goals within financial constraints (p.21).
The transformation priorities a "Urgent and Emergency Care Programme" and "Waste Reduction Programme" are tangible delivery vehicles (p.25).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
1.5, 6.6 (Page 3, 23): The plan demonstrates adaptability in its own creation. It  states that a contract offer received after the drafting period will "materially change" the submission in March, showing a responsive planning process to new funding realities.
3.1-3.3 (Page 10): The delivery approach is built on "close working relationships with System partners" to co-develop "demand mitigation schemes," indicating a flexible strategy that adapts to system-wide challenges and opportunities.
5.5.1, 5.5.4 (Page 18): The workforce plan acknowledges that "reconfiguration of services... will lead to different workforce models" and that "investment decisions support workforce development and sustainability," showing an adaptable approach to workforce planning based on service change.
Risks (Page 27-28): The comprehensive risk register, with identified mitigations for each major risk (e.g., no contract offer, WRP development, data quality), demonstrates a capacity to adapt plans in response to potential challenges.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
2.1.4, 2.1.8 (Page 5): The plan  acknowledges the shift to "moving care closer to home" and notes that at "Blackpool Place, partners are working together... to develop neighbourhood health models locally."
3.4 (Page 10): Actions to "improve the utilisation of Care Coordination, virtual wards and frailty initiatives" are designed to reduce UEC demand by providing care in community settings.
5.8.2 (Page 20): The workforce strategy details a "Shift to Community and Out-of-Hospital Care" by increasing community nursing capacity and developing "integrated neighbourhood teams."
7.3 (Page 25): The plan commits to "strengthening prevention and early intervention, improving access to community‑based alternatives to hospital care," enabling the 'left shift'.
DELIVERY: • Achieve break-even (financial balance) without deficit support.
• Implement new divisional structure (5 to 3 divisions).
• Develop sustainable talent pipelines aligned to local demographics.
• Begin c. 1,100 WTE headcount reduction (over 3 years).
• Embed new workforce models from service reconfiguration.
• Full implementation of new workforce models.
• Further improve performance against national targets.
• Continue progress towards full constitutional standards.
• Sustain performance against all constitutional standards.
• Introduce new SPOA models in two high-volume specialties.
• Embed demand management into core pathways.
• Deliver "outpatients as we know it" transformation.
• Improve RTT, 62-day cancer, diagnostic, and A&E 4-hour performance vs. Oct 2025.
• Implement single PACS/RIS solution with partners.
• Mature digital infrastructure (EPR, Federated Data Platform) driving productivity and population health management.
• Further integrate Federated Data Platform modules.
• Transfer pathology services to Lancashire Teaching Hospitals (1 April).
• Participate in service reconfiguration (e.g., vascular, orthodontics) as per system blueprint.
• Fully integrated system pathways, with resources shifted closer to home.
• Collaborate on demand growth reduction schemes with ICB.
• Explore One LSC shared corporate functions.
• Deliver Neighbourhood Health models at Blackpool Place.
analysis.DELIVERY_SHORT: No high-level plan provided, so presumed to be picked up in 3 year numerical plans.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
4.1 (Page 11-15): This section provides a comprehensive table of digital delivery areas, directly addressing the "analogue to digital" shift. Priorities include:
- Patient Engagement Portal: To improve communication and reduce 'Did Not Attend' (DNA) rates.
- Ambient Voice Technology: To enhance clinical productivity (citing a 14% gain from a pilot).
- Federated Data Platform: To standardise validation and improve pathways.
- People Digital Programme: To automate joiners/movers/leavers processes.
1.9 (Page 4): Confirms that digital transformations are foundational, underpinning performance, workforce, and financial plans.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
1.3, 2.2.6 (Page 3, 6): The plan repeatedly grounds itself in the context of serving "one of the most deprived populations in England" with the "lowest" male healthy life expectancy. This demonstrates that addressing inequality is a core driver, not an add-on.
2.1.8 (Page 5): It states that partners are "working together with a shared focus on reducing inequalities," showing system-level commitment.
5.2 (Page 16-17): The workforce section provides a detailed analysis of local deprivation, unemployment, and low skills levels, framing these as barriers to both health and employment that the trust, as a major employer, must address.
9.1 (Page 29): The plan has a mandatory "Equality and Quality Impact Assessment (EQIA/QIA)" process for all transformation programmes, ensuring equity is formally considered and risks are mitigated.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
4.1 (Page 11-15): The digital roadmap is a core innovation strand, including system-wide projects like a single EPR, LIMS, and a federated data platform, which are transformative for care delivery and efficiency. "BTH is also currently in receipt of significant NHS frontline digitisation funds, it is one of only 6 trusts nationally not have an EPR. The anticipated rollout out of further frontline digitation e.g. Ambient Dictation (planned in 26/27) and the procurement and deployment of an EPR is expected to support material changes to both how the current and future workforce operates and is deployed but our short- and medium-term resource to enable this hugely complex delivery." (p.17)
7.1 (Page 24): The plan outlines a dual approach to transformation: "a mature Quality Improvement (QI) methodology and a delivery focused Programme Management Office (PMO)." This provides a structured, evidence-based engine for change.
7.2 (Page 24-25): Transformation priorities are clearly defined, including a "Waste Reduction Programme" using rigorous governance and a commitment to "GIRFT-aligned service reviews" to reduce unwarranted variation.
2.1.7 (Page 5): The agreement to merge pathology services across four trusts is a tangible example of system-level service transformation to improve efficiency.
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
2.1.4 (Page 5): The plan highlights the system's focus on "disease prevention and earlier intervention" as a core strategic shift, aligning with the 10 Year Plan.
3.4 (Page 10): Specific "demand management" actions are prevention-focused, such as:
•	Working with primary care providers to remove unwarranted variation in GP referrals.
•	Improving the utilisation of Care Coordination, virtual wards and frailty initiatives in order to reduce UEC demand.
5.8.1 (Page 20): The workforce strategy  supports the "Shift to Prevention and Population Health" through "expansion of public health, community, and primary care roles" and "development of health coaching and social prescribing capacity."
7.2 (Page 25): The "Our Population" pillar prioritises "prevention and early detection, especially for disadvantaged groups, reducing avoidable demand."
analysis.SUMMARY:
Detailed understanding of its specific population c.350,000. analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
The plan  states that delivering the financial control totals requires a significant trade-off in workforce, with a "c. 1,100 WTE headcount reduction over the first 3-year period" (p.4) as part of a £38.5m Waste Reduction Programme. 
It demonstrates a shift in financial resources by planning to move from a £19.9m deficit in 26/27 to a break-even position in 27/28, implying a reallocation of resources from deficit support to frontline or transformation funding (p.23).
Section 7.3: The plan discusses a system-level resource shift, aiming to "shift resources safely into community settings" through collaborative pathway redesign, directly addressing the "hospital to community" trade-off.
analysis.VISION:
Our mission
To deliver safe, effective, sustainable care for everyone, everyday.
Our vision
We will improve the lives of people who live, work and volunteer on the Fylde Coast and beyond.
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
5.7.2 (Page 19): The strategic workforce vision includes a pillar on "Flexible and Integrated Working," promoting "multidisciplinary teams" and "system-wide workforce planning," which are fundamental changes in ways of working.
5.8.2, 5.9.1 (Page 20-21): The plan outlines new workforce models, including "integrated neighbourhood teams" and "optimised skill mix and role redesign" (e.g., expansion of advanced practitioners), moving away from traditional siloed working.
5.3.1 (Page 17): It acknowledges cultural challenges, such as having "one of the highest sickness in the NHS," and outlines mitigations like "Sickness Clinics" and a "culture improvement plan" (2.4.7, Page 9).
5.10.3 (Page 22): A unique point is the recognition of the trust's role in influencing the local economy and considering "wider metrics such as local unemployment rates, literacy rates" as part of its workforce responsibility.

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Plan states it is aligned to 10 Year Health Plan three shifts: hospital to community, analogue to digital, sickness to prevention (p. 6). Directly supports Greater Manchester system priorities and Bolton Partnership Locality Plan through neighbourhood delivery, prevention, and integrated pathways (p. 48-53).
" improving productivity and reducing inefficiency to sustain safety, effectiveness and experiences
 redesigning services to support neighbourhood delivery and care closer to home
 reducing unwarranted variation and improving flow to support access standards
 enabling prevention, demand management and better population health outcomes
 strengthening workforce sustainability through better use of skills, roles and capacity
 supporting digital and data enabled ways of working that reduce administrative burden and waste
 providing clear grip, control and assurance through a structured, multiyear improvement framework" (p.6)
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Phased trajectory delivers 92% Referral to Treatment (RTT), 85% Accident & Emergency (A&E), 1% diagnostics, and cancer standards by 2028/29 (p. 10). Four approved community business cases (Hospital at Home, Integrated Transfer of Care Hub, Enhanced Palliative Care, Proactive Care) demonstrate tangible left shift (p. 52-53). Financial breakeven by 2028/29 enables sustainable transformation (p. 4). System dependencies on primary care, social care, and Integrated Care Board (ICB) capacity remain critical (p. 54-57, 62).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Comprehensive risk log with early warning indicators, controls, and escalation routes (p. 59-66). Sensitivity analysis tests downside scenarios (e.g., 90% Cost Improvement Programme delivery, demand 1-2% above plan) with management responses (p. 56-58). Monthly portfolio reviews, quarterly deep dives, and stop/reset triggers (p. 55).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Bolton has six integrated neighbourhoods with multi-agency Integrated Neighbourhood Teams (INTs) (p. 51). Four approved business cases: Hospital at Home (58 virtual ward beds), Integrated Transfer of Care Hub (ITOCH), Enhanced Palliative Care at Home, Proactive Care Team (PACT) (p. 52-53). Community nursing, therapy services, and urgent community response delivered through neighbourhood footprints (p. 52).
DELIVERY: • Mature Patient Initiated Follow Up (PIFU), Advice and Guidance, digital follow-up.
• Enhanced flow and reduced length of stay.
• Reduce ambulance handovers to 38.6% in 15 mins (p. 10).
• Reduce ambulance handovers to 21.9% in 15 mins (p. 10).
• Reduce ambulance handovers to 5% in 15 mins (p. 10).
• Digital optimisation across imaging and pathology.
• Hospital at Home (58 virtual ward beds) established (p. 52).
• Integrated Transfer of Care Hub (ITOCH) established (p. 52).
• Outpatient activity shifted to community settings.
• Enhanced Palliative Care at Home service developed (p. 53).
• Workforce change synchronised with redesigned models.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Optimise Electronic Patient Record (EPR) to improve data quality and income capture. Implement Patient Initiated Follow Up (PIFU), Advice and Guidance, digital triage, virtual consultations. Strengthen interoperability with Greater Manchester Care Record. Deploy digital scheduling, utilisation dashboards, and bed management tools (p. 40-41).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
Bolton Prevention and Inequalities Framework and Bolton Outcomes Framework aligned to Joint Strategic Needs Assessment (JSNA) and Core20PLUS5 priorities (p. 51). Targeted action for populations experiencing greatest disadvantage:
"Six integrated neighbourhoods are established across Bolton, bringing together primary care, community services, adult social care, mental health services and voluntary, community and faith sector partners. Delivery is underpinned by the Bolton Prevention and Inequalities Framework and the Bolton Outcomes Framework, aligned to the JSNA and Core20PLUS5 priorities, ensuring targeted action for populations experiencing the greatest disadvantage." (p.51)
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Four approved community business cases (Hospital at Home, Integrated Transfer of Care Hub, Enhanced Palliative Care, Proactive Care) represent significant service innovation (p. 52-53). Strategic partner (PwC) to accelerate Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) and transformation (p. 46-47). Digital innovations include Patient Initiated Follow Up (PIFU), Advice and Guidance, digital triage, and automation (p. 40-41).
OTHER: Four approved community business cases (Hospital at Home, Integrated Transfer of Care Hub, Enhanced Palliative Care, Proactive Care) directly shift investment from hospital to community (p. 52-53).
Quality Management System (QMS) provides single framework linking strategy, improvement, delivery, and assurance (p. 35-39).
System Decision Log tracks system-dependent actions (Integrated Care Board (ICB), local authority, community capacity) with deadlines and escalation routes (p. 62).
Deep-dive on loss-making services using Service Line Reporting (SLR) and Patient Level Information and Costing System (PLICS) to develop evidence-based improvement plans (p. 29-31).
Integrated Neighbourhood Teams (INTs) with multi-agency membership deliver proactive care across six neighbourhoods (p. 51).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Neighbourhood-based Proactive Care Team (PACT) delivers anticipatory care for people with long-term conditions and frailty (p. 53). Population health management embedded using segmentation and risk stratification (p. 51-52). Anchor institution role includes tobacco dependency, healthy weight programmes (p. 18).
analysis.SUMMARY: High deprivation, stark life expectancy gaps, poor health literacy for diverse population of c.302,000. | aligned to three shifts and GM system priorities. | Phased trajectory credible, but success hinges on system partners delivering capacity. | Workforce reduction to fund community services and digital enablers. | Proactive Care Team and population health management embedded. | EPR optimisation and digital pathways to improve productivity. | Four approved business cases shift investment from hospital to community. | Redesigning roles with clinical leadership, not just headcount reduction. | Mandatory QEIAs ensure equity is assessed for all changes. | Strategic partner for capability; four new care models approved. | Risk log, sensitivity analysis, and stop/reset triggers enable course correction.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Workforce reduction of c. 850 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) over three years (p. 17-18) to deliver £77m Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) (p. 17). Hospital bed capacity reduced to fund community services (Hospital at Home, Integrated Transfer of Care Hub, etc.) (p. 52). Investment in digital and transformation enabled through savings.
analysis.VISION:
No vision or mission. Instead "a clear and credible approach to delivery, recovery and sustainability over the next three to five years, including:
- a coherent narrative for improvement and delivery across the medium term planning period, and a description of our planned performance against national standards over the next three years
- a realistic, staged trajectory to financial sustainability
- the improvement and transformation actions required to underpin that trajectory and enable delivery of the NHS Long Term Plan three shifts (hospital to community, sickness to prevention, analogue to digital)
- the Trust’s delivery approach through a Quality Management System (QMS) and integrated transformation model
- the governance, assurance and support required to deliver recovery while maintaining quality, access and safety
- The associated risks to delivery" (p.2)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Workforce change synchronised with redesigned care models, not standalone cost reduction (p. 44). Strengthen leadership capacity and clinical ownership of improvement. Build quality improvement, transformation, and digital capability. Improve job planning, rostering, skill-mix, and establishment control (p. 44-45). Strategic partner (PwC) to transfer capability (p. 46).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan  built around the three shifts: Hospital to Community, Treatment to Prevention, Analogue to Digital (p. 3). Framework shows relationship between Trust strategy, continuous improvement, transformation, and the 10 Year Health Plan (p. 8). Aligned to NHS Cheshire and Merseyside commissioning intentions (p. 62-67).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Comprehensive transformation plans across all care groups with specific Year 1-3 targets (p. 37-44). Digital shift well-advanced with detailed roadmap (p. 45-53). Hospital to Community shift supported by Crisis Assessment Centres, 24/7 Neighbourhood Mental Health Centres, and Physical Health Hubs (p. 54-56). Prevention embedded through Mental Health Support Teams, early intervention, and population health management (p. 3, 41-44).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) and corporate risk registers monitor strategic risks (p. 58). Specific risks identified with mitigations (workforce pressures, digital skills gaps, financial constraints, data quality) (p. 58-59). Strategy Deployment approach enables annual adjustment of breakthrough objectives (p. 9). Transformation plans phased across Years 1-3 with flexibility to respond to emerging priorities (p. 37-44, 46).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Hospital to Community is one of three core shifts (p. 3). Priorities include: 24/7 Neighbourhood Mental Health Centres, Crisis Assessment Centres (co-located with Emergency Departments), Physical Health Hubs in neighbourhoods, Learning Disability and Autism crisis accommodation, integrated neighbourhood teams, Virtual Wards, Community Response Hubs (p. 3, 44, 54-56).
DELIVERY: • Reduce Mental Health (MH) Accident & Emergency (A&E) attendances by 50% (p. 39).
• Reduce MH A&E attendances further to 6,160 (p. 40).
Integrated inpatient and community rehabilitation model.
• Reduce 12+ hour MH breaches in A&E by 50% (p. 39).
• Reduce adult length of stay to 42 days (p. 39).
• Reduce adult length of stay to 40 days (p. 40).
• Reduce Clinically Ready for Discharge (CRFD) to 5.0% (p. 39).
Learning Disabilities, Neurodevelopmental Disorders (NDD) and Acquired Brain Injury (ABI)
• 10% reduction in Learning Disability (LD) inpatient care (p. 37).
• Further 10% reduction in LD inpatient care (p. 38).
• Further 10% reduction in LD inpatient care (p. 38).
• 20% reduction in CRFD occupied bed days (p. 37).
• Further 10% reduction in CRFD bed days (p. 38).
• Further 10% reduction in CRFD bed days (p. 38).
• 10% reduction in autism assessment wait time (p. 37).
• Further 10% reduction in autism wait time (p. 38).
• 75% of ADHD service users report positive outcomes (p. 38).
• Adult Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) waits: 92% <52 weeks (p. 37).
• Reduce >60 day Tier 4 length of stay by 1% (p. 41).
• Reduce >60 day Tier 4 length of stay by 5% (p. 42).
New inpatient model aligned to national specification.
• 20% improvement in community services (excl NDD) against 18-week target (p. 41).
• Integrated 0-19 services across Cheshire and Merseyside (C&M) (p. 42).
Proactive care preventing 30% of acute episodes.
• Scale Community Response Hubs to 7-day operation (p. 44).
• Expand Wayfinder to 80% of suitable teams (p. 49).
• Full Trust coverage of standardised phones/devices (p. 51).
Single Electronic Patient Record (EPR) foundations; full digital maturity.
• Explore integration of Crisis Tool with SystmOne (p. 50).
• Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) reduction from efficiency: 150 (p. 28).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Comprehensive digital roadmap (p. 45-53). Priorities include: Electronic Patient Record (EPR) optimisation, Ambient Voice Technology (AVT), Federated Data Platform (FDP) crisis tool, digitised prescribing (ePMA/EPS), patient portal (Wayfinder/PKB), automation (Blue Prism), Mental Health Act digitisation (Thalamos), dashboards for clinical decision-making, workforce digital literacy.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: "Improving health equity and reducing inequalities" is a core improvement theme (p. 3, 7, 9). Breakthrough objective: all services have access to population insights to develop person-centred services (p. 10, 19). Priorities include: improving access in deprived communities, earlier intervention for children and young people, improving life expectancy for people with learning disabilities and serious mental illness, physical health checks, health inequality metrics in Integrated Performance Report (IPR) (p. 19).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: "CWPi" is the Trust-wide system for continuous improvement, involvement, innovation and learning, aligned to NHS IMPACT (p. 7, 20). Strategy Deployment (Hoshin Kanri) translates strategic ambitions into breakthrough objectives (p. 9). Transformation methods include Rapid Process Improvement Weeks (RPIW), Priority Programme Collaborative, and improvement coaching (p. 36). Digital innovations: Federated Data Platform (FDP) crisis tool, Ambient Voice Technology (AVT), automation, Thalamos, Limbic AI in Talking Therapies (p. 45-53).
OTHER: CWPi (continuous improvement system) aligned to NHS IMPACT, embedding quality management across the organisation (p. 7, 20).
Strategy Deployment (Hoshin Kanri) translates strategic themes into 3-5 year targets and 12-18 month breakthrough objectives (p. 9).
Productivity gains already delivered: 63% reduction in non-clinical agency, 55 days average adult acute length of stay (from 67 days), 50% reduction in 12-hour waits for mental health beds (p. 16).
Lead Provider Collaboratives (LPCs) for Tier 4 Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS), perinatal mental health, adult eating disorders, and adult secure services (p. 5, 14).
Mersey Care partnership with Joint Programme Board to secure delegation of mental health programme budget and expand standardised pathways (p. 14).
Dynamic system modelling with Whole Systems Partnership to reduce length of stay and eliminate independent sector bed use (p. 16).
Digital Crisis Care Coordination Tool on Federated Data Platform (FDP) – first mental health tool on FDP (p. 17, 50).
Quality and Equality Impact Assessment (QEIA) Panel meets weekly to review all efficiency schemes (p. 68).
Integrated Performance Report (IPR) monitors key plan metrics, reported across Committee structure (p. 60).
Capital investment of £29.8m secured for Mental Health Crisis Assessment Centres and 24/7 Neighbourhood Mental Health Hubs (p. 25).
Glossary included (p.70-77)
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Treatment to Prevention is one of three core shifts (p. 3). Priorities include: Mental Health Support Teams in schools, redesigned 0-5 services, integrated 16-25 support, risk stratification tool with General Practitioner (GP) populations, early intervention in psychosis, population health management (p. 3, 41-44). Really comprehensive and well laid out consideration of each shift.
analysis.SUMMARY: Covers 1m+ across Cheshire, Merseyside; high deprivation, poor health outcomes. | Four improvement themes directly mapped to three national shifts. Really strong alignment - clear how every action fits in the bigger picture. | Foundational shifts embedded; success depends on system-wide plan execution and funding. | WTE reduction to fund digital and community infrastructure. | MHSTs, 0-5 services, risk stratification with GPs. | Comprehensive roadmap: AVT, FDP crisis tool, EPR optimisation, automation. | 24/7 NMHCs, Crisis Hubs, Physical Health Hubs in neighbourhoods. | Just culture, top-quartile ambition, anti-racist employer commitment. | Core20PLUS5 focus; population insights to close accessibility gaps. Actively addressing anti-racism. | Detailed transformation and productivity plan. CWPi system; Strategy Deployment; Lead Provider Collaboratives. | BAF, risk mitigations, annual breakthrough objective refresh.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Efficiency plan of £13.13m (p. 26). Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) reduction of staff via from efficiency schemes (p. 28). Bank and agency reductions applied (p. 27). Capital investment prioritised for left-shift enablers: Crisis Assessment Centres, Neighbourhood Mental Health Centres, digital transformation (p. 25).
analysis.VISION: Helping to improve the lives of everyone in our communities.	p. 1, 6
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Workforce strategy focuses on compassionate, inclusive, psychologically safe culture (p. 3, 32-35). "Leading for the Future" framework supports three shifts through role redesign, digital skills, community-based teams, and prevention-focused roles (p. 32). Distributed leadership model, coaching styles, team self-management (p. 32). Anti-racist employer commitment, staff survey triangulation, Board appraisal with 360° feedback (p. 34).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Strategy refreshed to align with 10YHP three shifts and NHS C&M Five-year Strategic Commissioning Plan (p. 1, 5).  references shifts throughout delivery tables (p. 9-27).
-Transform the Clinical Decisions Unit (CDU), Urgent Emergency Care (UEC) and Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) pathways for cancer patients (p.14).
-Digital shift - workforce efficiencies including; AI workflow adoption, robotic process automation (p.29)
-utilising the hotline service to refer patients to SDECs who would previously have attended system EDs (p.3)
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Achieves all cancer standards (62-day 85%, 31-day 96%, 28-day 80%) from year one, exceeding national trajectories (p. 33). RTT at 97% vs 92% target.
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Rolling five-year plan refreshed annually (p. 2, 37). Board Assurance Framework with 12 strategic risks (p. 34-35). Responsive to novel therapies and AI advancements (p. 2).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Clinical Decisions Unit - CDU/SDEC/UEC pathways for cancer patients to relieve A&E pressure (p. 14). Chemotherapy at home, self-administration, remote monitoring all support left shift (p. 11, 24).
DELIVERY: • Validated risk tool to reduce adverse events by 20% by 2031 (p. 21).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Comprehensive digital agenda: single EPR across Liverpool (p. 24), AI algorithms (p. 22, 25), remote monitoring (p. 24), patient portal (p. 25), FDP/Cancer 360 (p. 25), ambient voice technology (p. 25).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Core20PLUS5 embedded: target staff in deprived areas for recruitment (p. 17). Health inequalities programme (p. 13). Research into frailty, deprivation, and cancer outcomes (p. 21). QEIA process for all schemes (p. 38).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Research leadership: CAR T, TIL therapy (p. 12-13), ECMC, early-phase trials (p. 19). AI in pathology/radiology (p. 22). Subsidiaries (PharmaC, Propcare) and PPJV private clinic (p. 27).
OTHER: Research as strategic driver: 40+ WTE research growth funded by commercial income, not core budgets (p. 20, 29).
Ambicare inpatient transformation: Releases bed capacity for novel therapies with minimal additional staffing (p. 11-12).
Single EPR collaboration: Partnership with five Liverpool acute providers to improve safety and information sharing (p. 6, 24).
NHS IMPACT alignment: Formalising quality improvement approach in line with national framework (p. 9).
NOF self-assessment: Rated "green" against The Insightful Provider Board criteria, demonstrating delivery capability (p. 37).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Health inequalities and anchor institution programme strengthens prevention (p. 13). Research into frailty, deprivation, and co-morbidity aims to reduce adverse events by 20% (p. 21).
analysis.SUMMARY: 2.7m across Cheshire & Merseyside; cancer incidence up 31% by 2040 (p. 1, 5). | Strategy refreshed to embed three shifts and system plans (p. 1, 5). | Exceeds all cancer targets from year one; enables focus on innovation and research (p. 33). | Research growth offsets CIP workforce reductions, funded by commercial income. | Frailty and deprivation research aims to reduce adverse events 20%. | Single EPR across Liverpool; AI, remote monitoring, patient portal. | CDU/SDEC pathways divert cancer patients from A&E | Leadership framework, inclusion actions, proactive wellbeing model | Core20PLUS5 recruitment; QEIAs for all schemes | CAR T, TIL therapy; subsidiaries and private clinic generate profit | Rolling five-year plan refreshed annually; Board Assurance Framework with 12 risks
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: CIP workforce reduction of 41.66 WTE over three years (p. 28), offset by research growth funded by commercial income (p. 29). Productivity gains enable new services with marginal investment.
analysis.VISION:
No specific vision mentioned - instead 6 strategic objectives: 
• Be outstanding
• Be collaborative
• Be a great place to work
• Be research leaders
• Be digital
• Be visionary
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Strategic objectives: Leadership Excellence, Cultural & Equity, Sustainability & Productivity (p. 16-18). NHS Leadership Framework adoption, HIAs, psychological safety, BME leadership gap closure, proactive wellbeing model (p. 16-18).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Corporate strategy 'Transforming Care Together' and Clinical Strategy 'Improving Lives'  aligned to 10YHP three shifts: hospital to community, analogue to digital, treatment to prevention (p. 2).
P.2 detail:
Treatment to Prevention
•Using population health data, we will deploy our collective resources to target populations most at risk through frailty and health inequalities. .
•Quality, safety and patient and family experience – we will continue to reduce harms, falls, and effective management of sepsis and improve the patient and family experience.
Hospital to Community / Home
•Operational transformation - We have specific plans to deliver constitutional waiting time standards Inc., urgent and planned care. We will bring forward transformational changes in service delivery in areas such as outpatients, digital opportunities and working in a more integrated way with partners.
Analogue to Digital
• We will continue our data and digital maturity and deploy operational AI solutions for waiting list validation, clinical coding and ambient voice technology (AVT). And we will pursue further clinical AI applications building on existing products for dermatology, stroke and surgery scheduling.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Phased trajectory: RTT 67%→92%, A&E 65.1%→85.2%, DM01 14%→1% by 2028/29. Cancer standards met throughout. UEC improvement requires system-level action on NCTR and bed occupancy (p. 23, 25-26).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework with 10 strategic risks regularly reviewed. Phased UEC investment (years 2-3). CIP identified vs. unidentified tracked. Transformation Board established year 1 (p. 48-50, 55).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: CDC bid to transfer 34,000+ diagnostic tests annually to community. Ellesmere Port CDC model success. Virtual wards, remote monitoring. Integrated community teams aligned to PCN footprints (p. 13-14, 30).
DELIVERY: • Targeted investment in 7-day working, extended roles.
• AVT phase 1, waiting list validation AI (p. 29).

analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Mature digital maturity: EPR, FDP (pilot site), AI (waiting list validation, clinical coding, AVT), Patient engagement portal (PEP) linked to NHS App, robotics, data science. Cardiology, ophthalmology, UEC predictive analytics planned (p. 27-29).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
Targeted support in high-inequality areas: Blacon, Chester City, Ellesmere Port using CIPHA data. Focus on patients with 4+ LTCs at highest admission risk. Primary care co-horted clinics (p. 14).
Combined Intelligence for Population Health Action (CIPHA) data used to target community services to patients with 4+ LTCs at highest admission risk (p. 14).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Geothermal project (first NHS Net Zero Building Standard for Women's & Children's Building). GIRFT pathway redesign, data-driven benchmarking (MHS, HED), theatre/diagnostic efficiency programmes (p. 18-21, 45-47).
OTHER: Geothermal project provides low-carbon heat, reducing energy volatility and supporting net zero (p. 47).
Women's & Children's Building first NHS facility to achieve NHS Net Zero Building Standard (p. 46).
Chester Medical School partnership aims for university hospital status through joint appointments and research (p. 12).
Anchor Institution Group includes Chester Zoo, Land Trust, local voluntary groups to improve population health (p. 12).
Transformation Board established year 1 to oversee clinical pathway redesign and efficiency programmes (p. 51, 55).
GIRFT governance group tracks adoption of best practice and benefits realisation across specialties (p. 15).
Work on deficit drivers (p.38)
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
Primary care educational events on cardiovascular/cardiometabolic disease (hypertension, diabetes, CKD). Targeted support in high-inequality areas using CIPHA data. Anchor Institution Group with Chester Zoo (p. 12, 14).
NEIGHBOURHOOD ALIGNED TO PLACE: "To support earlier intervention, prevention and admission avoidance we will continue our pro-active leadership approach with primary and community care and the development of integrated neighbourhood health teams." (P.12)
analysis.SUMMARY: Ageing population with widening life expectancy gap. | Strategies  built around three core shifts. | Phased RTT 92%, A&E 85% by 2029; system-wide UEC action critical. | Workforce reductions now fund later UEC investment. | Targeting high-risk groups with primary care education. | Advanced AI, FDP deployment and automation. | CDC expansion shifting diagnostics to community. | New roles, digital skills, and wellbeing focus. | Data-driven targeting of deprivation hotspots. | Geothermal and Net Zero building exemplars. | Board risk framework with phased investment responses.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Workforce reduction of 245 WTE (26/27) to deliver £25m CIP, offset by targeted UEC investment in years 2-3 (7-day working, extended roles). Bed occupancy must reduce 10% to meet targets (p. 26, 41).
analysis.VISION: Our Corporate strategy `Transforming Care Together` and Clinical Strategy `Improving Lives` aligns with the Ten-Year Plan and Cheshire and Merseyside Provider Collaborative Blueprint and provides the platform that empowers clinical and operational staff to not only adapt to change but to shape it, ensuring the delivery of high-quality patient care, improved outcomes, and sustainable growth. p.2
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
People Strategy, ACP/ANP expansion, skill mix optimisation, electronic rostering, wellbeing focus, apprenticeship routes. Digital navigators, service content owners to support adoption (p. 30-32).
Strategic vision of trust's approach to workforce: 
•Strengthening local recruitment and apprenticeship routes to build reliable, home-grown talent pipelines and reduce long-term workforce supply risks.
•Providing clear progression routes into advanced and specialist roles to support retention, role fulfilment and organisational capability.
•Expanding flexible working options and enabling portfolio careers to enhance work–life balance, improve wellbeing, and retain experienced staff.
•Investing in leadership development and modernising statutory and mandatory training, supporting compassionate leadership, high-quality supervision, and positive organisational behaviours.
•Improving staff wellbeing and reducing sickness absence through targeted health and wellbeing support, improved access to interventions, and proactive absence management.

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Strategy aligned to 10 Year Health Plan's three shifts through pillar one (p.2). Aligns with Cheshire and Merseyside Integrated Care System (ICS) objectives and Integrated Care Partnership strategy (p. 3). Developed through extensive engagement with over 11,000 responses (p. 3).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Prevention, community, and digital priorities embedded. However, financial sustainability and service fragility depend on system-level solutions through NHS England and C&M ICB taskforce (p. 8-9). Workforce plans not yet mature due to system-level dependencies (p. 9).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Annual plans aligned to governance framework; projects prioritised annually and emergently (p. 5, 7). Risks identified: service fragility, workforce plan maturity, infrastructure limitations, financial sustainability (p. 9). Engagement with NHS England and C&M ICB taskforce on sustainability (p. 8-9).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Communities and Partnerships" is third strategic pillar (p. 1, 4). Priorities include: Integrated Neighbourhood Team (INT) approach, place-based partnerships, expanded community services, alternatives to hospital admission, integration of mental health and physical health pathways, co-production with communities (p. 4).
DELIVERY: • Embed Making Every Contact Count approach (p. 3).
• Strengthen neighbourhood and integrated care models (p. 3).
• Use risk stratification to identify at-risk populations (p. 3).
• Deliver 'left shift' of resources from acute to community (p. 3).
• Reduce delays in diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up (p. 4).
• Strengthen communication and continuity of care (p. 4).
• Consistent, high-quality care across all pathways.
Sustained reduction in harm and variation.
• Expand same-day emergency care and virtual wards (p. 4).
• Strengthen place-based partnerships within Cheshire East (p. 4).
• Expand community-based services and alternatives to admission (p. 4).
• Mature INT model with multi-agency working (p. 4).
• Improve integration of mental health and physical health pathways (p. 4).
• Continue sickness absence reduction towards 4.3% by 2029 (p. 5).
• Strengthened management capability and early intervention (p. 5).
• Use AI and automation to support productivity (p. 5).
• Reduce reliance on paper and manual processes (p. 5).
• Strengthen cyber security and digital infrastructure (p. 5).
• Expand patient access through NHS App and digital portal (p. 5).
• Work with NHS England and C&M ICB taskforce on service sustainability (p. 8-9).
• Implement service redesign outputs from taskforce (p. 8-9).
Financially sustainable model agreed and implemented.
• Deliver efficiency schemes through Innovation and Productivity Group (IPG) (p. 7).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: "Connected and Digital" is fifth strategic pillar (p. 1, 4-5). Digital Clinical System (DCS) implemented June 2025 (p. 5, 8). Priorities include: DCS optimisation, reducing paper processes, expanding NHS App and digital portal access, AI and automation, strengthened cyber security, digital inclusion (p. 5).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Preventative and Proactive pillar includes targeted interventions for Core20PLUS5 populations (p. 3). Strategy acknowledges persistent health inequalities despite higher average life expectancy (p. 2). Anchor institution role and NHS Prevention Pledge commitment support action on wider determinants (p. 3).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Innovation and Productivity Group (IPG) oversees transformation (p. 7). Quality Improvement (QI) function provides structured, evidence-based methods (p. 7). Use of benchmarking tools (Model Hospital, GIRFT, NHS England Productivity packs) (p. 7). Quality Impact Assessment (QIA) and Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) panel for all schemes (p. 7).
OTHER: New Digital Clinical System (DCS) implemented June 2025, enabling real-time information, electronic prescribing, and advanced analytics (p. 5, 8).
Over 11,000 engagement responses received during strategy development, including more than 10,000 from patients (p. 3).
Four Dimensional (4D) quality improvement framework embedded to support continuous improvement (p. 4).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: "Preventative and Proactive" is first strategic pillar (p. 1, 3). Priorities include: Making Every Contact Count, targeted interventions for Core20PLUS5 populations, expansion of community diagnostics and screening, neighbourhood models, use of risk stratification, anchor institution role, NHS Prevention Pledge commitment (p. 3).
analysis.SUMMARY: Cheshire East; higher average life expectancy masks significant localised deprivation. | Five pillars  mirror the three national shifts. | Strategy sound, but financial sustainability hinges on system-level solutions and taskforce outcomes. | Moving from short-term savings to system-wide transformation and partnership working. | First pillar; Core20PLUS5, MECC, community diagnostics, anchor institution. | New DCS implemented; focus on optimisation, NHS App, AI, and inclusion. | INT model; place-based partnerships; alternatives to admission. | Sickness target 4.3% by 2029; digital literacy; interdisciplinary roles. | Core20PLUS5 targeted; acknowledges hidden inequalities despite higher average life expectancy. | IPG; QI function; QIA/EQIA panel for all schemes. | Annual planning; system taskforce engagement; risks on service fragility and infrastructure.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Acknowledges need for "step-change in productivity, pathway redesign and reduction of unwarranted variation" rather than short-term savings (p. 6). Financial sustainability requires system-level transformation and partnership working (p. 6). Sickness absence reduction target from 5.2% to 4.3% by 2029 (p. 5).
analysis.VISION: "To deliver outstanding care and to improve the health of the people we serve."	p. 3
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Recognises need for flexibility, digital literacy, interdisciplinary and community-based roles (p. 5). Priorities include: continuous learning culture, career development, apprenticeships, advanced practice, leadership development, domestic recruitment, wellbeing support, sickness absence reduction to 4.3% by 2029 (p. 5).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
p. 3, Executive Summary: The plan is  developed to "implement the ambitions of the 10 Year Health Plan."
p. 8, sec 2.2: It outlines how population health challenges (deprivation, inequitable access) directly shape the plan.
p. 38-40, sec 7.2: A dedicated table maps ELHT's plans directly to the ICB's vision, quadruple aims, strategic objectives, operating model, and the three 10YHP shifts (Sickness to Prevention, Care Closer to Home, Analogue to Digital). This shows a high degree of intentional alignment.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
p. 10-11, sec 3.1: The plan phases delivery into three periods: Year 1 (Stabilisation) focuses on foundational activities, eradicating corridor care, and exiting the Recovery Support Programme (RSP). Years 2-3 (Transforming) focuses on transforming care models and achieving a balanced exit run rate by March 2029.
p. 43, Risks: It acknowledges key delivery risks, such as the "affordability of delivering compliant plan" and "commissioning intentions for demand management not achieved," but assesses these with mitigations, suggesting a realistic view of deliverability challenges.
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
p. 42-44, sec 9. Risks and mitigations: A comprehensive risk register is provided, with each key risk (e.g., triangulation risk, commissioning intentions, health equity) assessed for likelihood and impact, and paired with specific, actionable mitigations. This demonstrates a structured approach to adapting to challenges.
p. 12-13, sec 3.2: The planning process itself is designed to be adaptable. It describes a "bottom-up" approach with service-level plans, executive "check and challenge" workshops, and triangulation of plans, allowing for course correction as plans are developed and reviewed.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
p. 3, Executive Summary: A key priority is to "work with primary care and our Place partners to provide care closer to home, through our neighbourhoods."
p. 5-6, sec 2.1: It provides evidence of success, citing the "Lancashire Model of Intermediate Care" and integrated neighbourhood teams (INTs) leading to decreased emergency admissions and re-admissions.
p. 36-37, sec 7.1: The plan details joint work at "Place" to fully embrace the "left shift," targeting specific wards with high health inequalities and designing local services around community hubs.
DELIVERY: • Generate financial surplus to support further transformation and investment.
• Assume £36.8m DSF, reducing to £27.5m net of transition.
• Colleagues recommend ELHT as a place to work.
• Net increase in WTE due to One LSC transfer and activity demands.
• Net reduction in WTE from WRP schemes.
• Net reduction in WTE from WRP schemes. Agency staff target 0.00 WTE.
• Continue strong performance against constitutional standards (RTT, cancer, diagnostics).
• Deliver benefits from care closer to home initiatives.
• Clear journey to achieving NOF Segment 2.
• One LSC lead provider role embedded (c. £88m additional income).
• Deliver joint Place-based plans targeting health inequalities.
• Form Integrated Health Organisation (IHO) with Place partners.
• Pathology service transfers to LTH (1 April).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
p. 20-22, sec 4.2: A comprehensive table outlines key digital priorities, including: a single ICS-wide EPR, Patient Engagement Portal (PEP) , Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) , single PACS/RIS, People Digital Programme, and an ICS wide data lake.
p. 20, sec 4.2: The digital strategy aims to be "digital by default, where it is safe to do so," and to free up clinical time through tools like AVT and robotic process automation.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
p. 3, Executive Summary: A core aim is to ensure "equitable access to services."
p. 6-7, sec 2.2: The plan provides granular detail on local health inequalities, noting that Burnley and Blackburn with Darwen are in the top 10 most deprived districts. It states that "health equity is central to how we plan and deliver care."
p. 8, sec 2.2: It acknowledges challenges like "inequitable access to care, including a growing digital divide."
p. 44, Risks: A specific "Health Equity" delivery risk is identified, with detailed mitigations including embedding health equity impact assessments, targeting neighbourhood-level interventions, and strengthening partnerships with VCFSE organisations.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
p. 3, Executive Summary: Ambitions include being a "robotic surgery centre of excellence" and a leader in "complex surgery."
p. 20-22, sec 4.2: Digital innovations like Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) and a single ICS-wide EPR are central to transforming clinical and administrative processes.
p. 35-37, sec 7.1: The plan details system-level transformation through the Lancashire and South Cumbria Provider Collaborative (PCB) , working on clinical reconfiguration (e.g., vascular, urogynaecology) and corporate transformation (One LSC).
p. 9, sec 3.1: The long-term ambition is to form an Integrated Health Organisation (IHO) with Place partners.
OTHER: 5-year, 3-phase transformation map ("Stabilisation," "Transforming," "Leading the Way") structures the delivery roadmap with specific milestones for each period (p. 10-11).

Embedded Improvement Practice (SPE+) methodology, which is combined with a Programme Management Office (PMO) to create a dual engine for continuous improvement and disciplined programme delivery (p. 7, 34).

ELHT plays an active role in the Lancashire and South Cumbria Provider Collaborative (PCB) , leading and participating in system-wide service reconfiguration (e.g., vascular, urogynaecology) and corporate transformation (One LSC) (p. 38-39).
A "Board to Board" style Performance and Improvement Review (PIR) process between Divisions and the Executive Team ensures close monitoring of divisional business plans and KPIs (p. 47).
board skills matrix  (p.45)
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
p. 3, Executive Summary: The plan's strategic priorities include "shifting to proactive, preventative and community-based care."
p. 7, sec 2.2: It  states the plan "prioritises health creation, prevention and early intervention" to reduce avoidable demand and improve long-term outcomes.
p. 10, sec 3.1: The principle "patients will only be in hospital when care cannot be provided in another setting" is a core preventative driver.
p. 37, sec 7.1: The "Urgent and Emergency Care Improvement" programme aims for upstream work on prevention, targeting "rising tide" users.
analysis.SUMMARY: Detailed understanding of its specific population 566,000 - focused on deprived Pennine Lancashire population | Mapped and aligned to all three shifts | Phased plan with acknowledged risks and mitigations | Shifts from deficit support to recurrent balance via WRP | Core principle driving upstream work and neighbourhood teams | Ambitious plan for EPR, AI, and data lake | Proven success with integrated neighbourhood teams | Multi-year focus on wellbeing, inclusion, and future skills | Central to strategy with dedicated risk and mitigations | Robotic surgery and system-wide reconfiguration via PCB | Comprehensive risk register with specific mitigations
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
p. 33, sec 6.2: The financial strategy  shows a shift in resources. It plans to transition from reliance on Deficit Support Funding (DSF) – assuming £36.8m in 26/27, reducing to £0.8m in 28/29 – to a balanced exit run rate, implying a shift from short-term support to recurrent financial sustainability.
p. 30, Workforce Table: The workforce plan shows a clear trade-off, with a significant increase in staff in Year 1 (due to One LSC transfer and activity demands), followed by net reductions in Years 2 and 3 as Waste Reduction Programme (WRP) schemes take effect.
analysis.VISION: "To be widely recognised for providing safe, personal and effective care."	p. 3
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
p. 27-28, sec 5.1: The plan prioritises putting "workforce first," being a "supportive, inclusive and compassionate organisation," and developing the "workforce for the future."
p. 28, sec 5.1: It outlines a multi-year workforce focus, from supporting wellbeing in Year 1, to empowering staff with improvement ideas in Years 2-3, to becoming a top-rated employer in Years 4-5.
p. 34, sec 7.1: It describes embedding its "Improvement Practice (SPE+)" at the heart of its culture, combining it with a Programme Management Office (PMO) for delivery.
p. 45, sec 10: It details a strengthened leadership and governance framework following external reviews, including a Board development programme.

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan aligned to three shifts: hospital to community, sickness to prevention, analogue to digital (p. 5, 14, 29). Aligned to Greater Manchester Integrated Care Strategy, Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy, and locality Neighbourhood Health Plans (p. 5-6). Three strategic aspirations directly mirror the shifts for different patient cohorts (p. 14).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Community shift advanced through Community Transformation Programme, Integrated Neighbourhood Teams, and 86-bed reduction over three years (p. 17-18, 38). Prevention embedded through Mental Health Support Teams (MHSTs) expansion to 100% by 2029, early intervention, and neighbourhood working (p. 17, 24). Digital shift progressing with joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme with Pennine Care and digital literacy plan (p. 27, 34). Zero inappropriate Out of Area Placements (OAPs) target by March 2027 (p. 24).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) with six strategic risks monitored by committees and Board (p. 48). Comprehensive risk register for plan delivery with mitigations (p. 49-52). Financial risks and mitigations modelled (p. 39). Quality and Equality Impact Assessment (QEIA) framework for all transformation and savings schemes (p. 53). Transformation programmes with Senior Responsible Officer (SRO) accountability and Programme Management Office (PMO) oversight (p. 16).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 5, 14). Priorities include: Community Transformation Programme, Integrated Mental Health Neighbourhood Teams (bringing together General Practitioner (GP), secondary care, social care, Voluntary, Community, Faith and Social Enterprise (VCFSE)), specialist neighbourhood mental health offer aligned to Greater Manchester (GM) Live Well approach, 24/7 Neighbourhood Mental Health Centres (subject to funding), and community first model of care (p. 14, 17-18).
DELIVERY: Eliminate inappropriate OAPs by March 2027 (p. 24). Never go above two OAPs in any month (p. 24).
Adult Acute and Psychiatric Intensive Care Unit (PICU) Length of Stay
Reduce average length of stay from 59 to 56 days (p. 24).
Reduce average length of stay from 155 to 130 days (p. 24).
Achieve 51% reliable recovery and 69% reliable improvement by March 2027 (p. 24).
• Sickness absence reduction to 5.6% by March 2029 (p. 31, 38).
• Remove approximately 86 inpatient beds over three years (p. 38).
• Sickness absence reduction from 7.0% to 6.0% by March 2027 (p. 31).
• Bank and agency three-point plan: strengthen control, reduce cost, reduce demand (p. 30).
• Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) impact of savings: 184 (p. 37).
• Remove 86 inpatient beds over three years (p. 38).
• Reduced unwarranted variation in enhanced observations (p. 40).
• Implement 'All in it Together' staff ideas scheme (p. 40).
• Corporate spend reduction (legal services, corporate functions) (p. 40).
• Space utilisation improvement and estate rationalisation (p. 40).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme with Pennine Care NHS Foundation Trust (p. 27). Digital aspirations include: digital front door for service users, electronic reported outcome measures, digital 'story' for service users to share with connected services, digital literacy improvement plan, accessibility standards (WCAG AA), and customer satisfaction (CSAT) measurement for digital services (p. 27, 34).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Three-year Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) plan with three priorities: become an anti-racist organisation, create equity in access, build capacity to thrive (p. 35). Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF) implementation to guide co-design of services with racially diverse communities (p. 26, 35). Targeted interventions including culturally adapted therapies, mobile clinics, and multiple access routes (digital, in-person, outreach, community-based) (p. 35). Data used to identify gaps in access and outcomes across protected characteristics (p. 35).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Three core transformation programmes: Community Transformation, Acute Transformation, Rehabilitation Services Transformation, each with Senior Responsible Officer (SRO) and Programme Management Office (PMO) support (p. 16-20). Rehabilitation transformation includes capital bid for 11 step-down rehabilitation flats (p. 19). Integrated Fund reinvests savings into left-shift initiatives (p. 38, 44). Joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme with Pennine Care (p. 27). 'All in it Together' staff ideas scheme for productivity (p. 40).
OTHER: Joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme with Pennine Care NHS Foundation Trust with board-level commitment to collaborate (p. 27).
86 inpatient beds removed over three years to fund community investment (p. 38).
Integrated Fund reinvests savings from reduced high-cost placements into left-shift initiatives (p. 38, 44).
Rehabilitation transformation includes capital bid for 11 step-down rehabilitation flats co-produced with service users and Voluntary, Community, Faith and Social Enterprise (VCFSE) partners (p. 19).
'All in it Together' scheme to gather staff ideas for productivity improvement (p. 40).
Bank and agency three-point plan (strengthen control, reduce cost, reduce demand) has delivered proven success (p. 30).
Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF) implementation to guide co-design with racially diverse communities (p. 26, 35).
Mental Health Support Teams (MHSTs) expanded to 100% coverage by 2029 (p. 24).
Anti-racist organisation commitment with North West Black, Asian and Minority Ethnic (BAME) Assembly accreditation (p. 32, 35).
Digital 'story' for service users to share with connected services, avoiding repeated retelling of trauma (p. 27).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: "Sickness to prevention" is a core shift (p. 5, 14). Priorities include: Mental Health Support Teams (MHSTs) in schools and colleges (to 100% by 2029), early intervention in psychosis, working with Voluntary, Community, Faith and Social Enterprise (VCFSE) and primary care in neighbourhoods to support people to remain well, and proactive access before crisis point (p. 14, 17, 24).
analysis.SUMMARY: 1.7m across five localities; high deprivation and diversity. | Three aspirations directly mirror national shifts. | Transformation programmes drive shift; bed reduction enables community reinvestment. | 86 beds removed to fund community investment. | MHSTs to 100% by 2029; early intervention focus. | Joint EPR with Pennine Care; digital front door. | Neighbourhood teams and Community Transformation Programme. | Anti-racist; bank/agency three-point plan. | PCREF and anti-racist accreditation. | Three transformation programmes with SROs; Integrated Fund. | BAF, QEIA framework, transformation governance.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Underlying deficit of £23.2m reduced to £9.6m by 2030/31 through savings programme averaging 4.8% of operating expenditure (p. 37). 86 inpatient beds removed over three years to fund community investment (p. 38). Agency reduction of 30% and bank reduction above 10% national target (p. 30, 40). Integrated Fund reinvests savings from reduced high-cost placements into 'left shift' initiatives (p. 38, 44).
analysis.VISION:
Our vision: Great places for care and great places to work
Our purpose: Working in partnership to improve the quality of life for all we serve (p.12)
Our enabling strategies: 
Care strategy
Research and Innovation strategy
People strategy
Together strategy
Digital strategy
Medium term financial plan
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: People Strategy with five priorities: wellbeing, inclusion, learning, digital, social value (p. 30). Anti-racist organisation commitment with North West Black, Asian and Minority Ethnic (BAME) Assembly accreditation (p. 32, 35). Sickness absence reduction plan targeting 5.6% by 2029 (p. 31). Bank and agency three-point plan (strengthen control, reduce cost, reduce demand) (p. 30). Digital literacy improvement plan (p. 34). Professional plans for Medical, Nursing, Allied Health Professionals (AHP), Psychological Professions (p. 44).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
p. 6:  states the plan "is aligned with the three big shifts as described in the 10yr Health Plan" and "aligns with the emerging Integrated Care Board operating model."
p. 6: Sets out system-agreed scenarios for Outpatients/GP referrals (high growth) and UEC/non-elective admissions (low growth), showing alignment with ICB planning.
p. 11: Workforce plan "supports delivery of the NHS 10-Year Plan priorities, including increased use of multidisciplinary working, continued use of advanced practice roles."
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
p. 8-9: Provides a phased delivery narrative for Years 1, 2 and 3, moving from "stabilisation" to "accelerated recovery" to "transition to sustained performance."
p. 7: Notes that A&E performance "remains constrained by flow and occupancy pressures, which are  recognised in the plan," showing realistic assessment.
p. 15: Quantifies the unprecedented scale of cost reduction required (£60m WRP in Year 1) and identifies this as "the single greatest delivery risk."
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
p. 3: States the plan "will be refreshed annually as part of the national planning cycle. Assumptions, trajectories and delivery actions will be reviewed and re-baselined."
p. 24-25, Risk Table: Comprehensive risk assessment with explicit scoring and mitigations for key risks (affordability, system demand, planning timetable, capacity modelling, health inequalities).
p. 26: Future strategic developments (pathology integration, theatre expansion, service reconfiguration) are "not assumed in the current plan" where benefits are uncertain, demonstrating a cautious, baseline approach.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
p. 4: Plan recognises that demand pressures require "system partnership working" and "sustained investment in prevention, neighbourhood-based and community interventions."
p. 6: At neighbourhood and place level, the Trust "works with primary care, community providers, local authorities and the VCFSE sector to support demand management, admission avoidance and improved discharge flow."
p. 10: Delivery of UEC standards depends on "neighbourhood and community capacity outside the Trust's control."
DELIVERY: • Reduce bank spend by 10% and agency spend by 30% (p. 10, 12).
• Sustainable, skilled workforce aligned to demand.
• Continue substantive WTE reduction via vacancy control.
• Stabilisation: Focus on backlog management (c.29k follow-up backlog) (p. 8).
• Accelerated recovery: Greater impact from Trust mitigations.
• Transition to sustained performance: Near-compliant RTT and cancer standards.
• Expand PIFU to "best in class +5%", reducing OPFups by c.21,072 (p. 7).
• Improved RTT, DM01 and cancer standards.
• Improved A&E performance, but constrained by flow.
• Expand digital outpatient processes (e-booking, digital letters) (p. 17).
• Increased use of FDP for data/analytics.
• Digitally mature organisation with AI-enabled workflows.
• Deploy digital workflow tools for WL management.
• Full benefits from digital productivity interventions.
• Mature Advice & Guidance and referral triage.
• Active membership in LSC Provider Collaborative (p. 21).
• Fully integrated health and care system.
• Reduced reliance on acute care through system working.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
p. 20, Table: Provides a detailed assessment of progress against national digital priorities, with all marked as "On target." Key priorities include:
• NHS App-first communications and appointments.
• Federated Data Platform (FDP) adoption.
• Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme (HIMSS Level 5).
• Ambient Voice Technology to reduce admin burden.
• Digital inclusion framework to mitigate exclusion.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
p. 4: Identifies "persistent inequalities in life expectancy, healthy life expectancy and cancer outcomes" and "higher-than-average prevalence of chronic disease."
p. 10: Quality and safety section states the plan "reflects population health need and health inequalities, including CORE20PLUS5 cohorts."
p. 12: Social Value Strategy embeds Health Inequalities Plan and Patient Experience Plan.
p. 25, Risk Table:  includes "Health Inequalities and Equity Considerations" as a risk, with mitigation including embedding equity into planning, pathway redesign and performance monitoring.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
Figure 3: Describes the Lancashire Improvement Method as a continuous improvement approach using data-driven insights and evidence-based practices, empowering staff to identify and implement changes.
Figure 6: Eight transformation themes (Workforce, Digital, Procurement, Model Service Reviews, Back-office, Drugs, Commercial, System-side) identify over £130m of opportunities.
p. 19: Ambient Voice Technology as an AI-powered tool to reduce admin burden and increase patient-facing time.
p. 26: Future developments include pathology collaborative integration and theatre/diagnostic transformation.
OTHER: Eight Transformation Themes provide a structured approach to identify over £130m of opportunities across workforce, digital, procurement and service redesign (p. 18).
Team Engagement and Development (TED) tool supports cultural change and healthy team dynamics during transformation (p. 12).
Delivery Area
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
p. 4: Population health assessment  links deprivation, chronic disease and increased service utilisation, driving the need for prevention.
p. 6: System-agreed UEC scenario is capped at 2.5% growth "predicated on effective system‑wide demand management, admission avoidance and improved flow."
p. 10: Delivery of UEC standards is reliant on "admission avoidance for frail and high‑risk cohorts" and "neighbourhood and community capacity."
analysis.SUMMARY: Focuses on Central Lancashire and wider LSC specialist population. | aligned to 10YHP three shifts and ICB system scenarios. | Phased delivery plan with realistic risk assessment and constraints. | Planned WTE reduction and resource shift via eight transformation themes. | UEC growth capped, reliant on system demand management and admission avoidance. | Comprehensive digital plan including NHS App, FDP, EPR and AI. | Relies on system partners for demand management and community capacity. | Anchor institution with retention focus, supported by culture and wellbeing plans. | CORE20PLUS5 cohorts identified, equity embedded in planning and risk management. | Lancashire Improvement Method and eight transformation themes drive innovation. | Annual refresh with explicit risk management and cautious baseline assumptions.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
p. 11: Workforce plan assumes a "progressive reduction in overall WTE over the five-year period, driven by service review, productivity improvement and more effective deployment of staff."
p. 13, Figure 4: Shows tangible reductions already achieved: 65% reduction in agency WTE, 18% reduction in bank WTE, reduction of c.200 substantive WTE.
p. 16: The eight transformation themes identify "over £130m of opportunities" to shift resources through service redesign and productivity.
analysis.VISION: To be a leading organisation providing safe, compassionate and high-quality care, and to be recognised for excellence in specialised and acute services.	p. 2
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
p. 12: Anchor institution role enables "local recruitment," "growing our own," "investment in apprenticeships" and "creating accessible career pathways."
p. 12: Acknowledges that "levels of transformation and change will be significant" and could impact "employee morale, health and wellbeing." Uses Team Engagement and Development (TED) tool and Management Development Programmes to support change.
p. 14: Comprehensive sickness improvement plan addresses prevention, education, digital tools, and grip and control.

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
p. 4, Executive Summary:  states that the "Ten-Year NHS plan is the framework for becoming Fit for the Future" and that the Trust has "mapped the key requirements from the Ten-Year Health Plan against the current Trust Strategy," finding its key points already included.
p. 5: The Quadruple Aim (Best possible care, Joy and Pride in Work, Improving Health, Value for money) and 12 Strategic Goals provide a framework that reflects the 10YHP's ambitions.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
p. 4, Executive Summary: Identifies four specific NHS Oversight Framework (NOF) areas for "rapid improvement" with "detailed plans... in place," including sickness absence, crisis response, long-stay discharges, and urgent community response.
p. 11-12: Provides a trajectory forecasting improvement from NOF Segment 4 to Segment 3 by Q1 2026/27. It also sets out 3-year trajectories for key 10YHP mental health metrics, such as reducing Adult Acute Length of Stay from 61.5 days to 40 days by March 2029 (p. 12).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
p. 33-34: A dedicated "Risks and mitigations" section outlines a comprehensive risk model, including a "Risk Management Strategy" approved in November 2025. A high-level risk analysis is provided at Appendix A (p. 39-43) , with mitigating actions for each key risk (e.g., health inequalities, workforce, finance).
p. 4, Executive Summary: States that "further service developments" will be brought into the "rolling 5 Year Plan," indicating a dynamic, evolving document.
p. 44-48, Appendix B: Uses iterative A3 summary sheets for key transformation programmes, which are designed to develop as understanding of challenges and solutions emerge.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
p. 4, 17, 25: The Executive Summary (p. 4) mentions "investment into community teams." The workforce plan (p. 17) notes work is "underway to explore what that transformation looks like for our workforce through the establishment of neighbourhood hubs." The transformation section (p. 25) states the Trust is "strengthening neighbourhood working as a core mechanism for delivering integrated, place‑based models of care."
p. 30-31: The Estates enabler plan details plans to develop "Community Roots' neighbourhood mental health centres" and "24/7 Neighbourhood MH Centres (NMHCs)" to deliver care on a neighbourhood footprint.
DELIVERY: • Sustainable financial balance through left-shift investment.
• Implement colleague attendance plan to reduce sickness (p. 16).
• Skilled, flexible workforce aligned to community models.
• Zero inappropriate Out of Area Placements (OAPs) from April (p. 11).
• Reduce Adult Acute LoS to 46 days (p. 12).
• Reduce Adult Acute LoS to 40 days (p. 12).
• Sustainable, high-quality community and inpatient services.
• Improve Urgent Community Response (2hr standard) to 92.71% (p. 11).
• Reduce Older Adult LoS to 102 days (p. 12).
• Reduce Older Adult LoS to 90 days (p. 12).
• Reduce Adult Acute LoS to 55 days (p. 12).
• Reduce Older Adult LoS to 118 days (p. 12).
• Integrate Patient Portal with NHS App (p. 14).
• Further embed FDP and digital interoperability (p. 14).
• Use LSCI methodology for key pathway redesigns (p. 44-48).
• Active membership in LSC Provider Collaborative (p. 26).
• Fully integrated health and care system.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
p. 14-15, Table: Provides a detailed adoption plan for core digital products, including integrating the Patient Engagement Portal with the NHS App by Q1 26/27, adhering to the Federated Data Platform (FDP) policy, and using Electronic Patient Records (EPRs).
p. 16, 29: The workforce plan highlights "Analogue to Digital" as a key theme, with plans for ambient voice technology and digital dictation in 2026/27. The Digital enabler plan (p. 29) outlines using AI (Microsoft Copilot, ambient dictation) to release time to care.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: p. 7-8: Provides a comprehensive overview of its approach, stating it is "embedding an equity lens into every core element of planning, transformation and operational delivery." Key actions include using population health intelligence, integrating health inequality objectives into appraisals, co-design with communities via VCFSE partnerships, and addressing wider determinants (e.g., homelessness, employment). This directly addresses the principles of CORE20PLUS5.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
p. 9-10, 25-26: The Trust's improvement methodology, LSCI (Lancashire and South Cumbria improvement approach) , is its core innovation framework for embedding continuous improvement. Transformation is delivered through an "integrated, system‑facing model" combining pathway redesign, value management, and neighbourhood working (p. 25).
p. 14, 29: Digital innovations include AI (Microsoft Copilot, ambient dictation) and the systematic adoption of core digital products like the NHS App and FDP.
OTHER: The LSCI improvement methodology (Lancashire and South Cumbria improvement approach) is the core, embedded framework for driving continuous improvement, reducing variation, and ensuring staff ownership of change (p. 9-10, 25).

A value management approach systematically reduces waste and focuses on the "productivity triangle" of inputs, outputs, and outcomes to ensure resources are directed to highest-value clinical activity (p. 27-28).
The Risk Management Strategy (approved Nov 2025) uses a mature model with risks tracked from ward to board via the IRIS system and Board Assurance Framework, ensuring integrated oversight (p. 33-34).
A3 problem-solving is used for key transformation programmes (e.g., Patient First Inpatient Pathway, CYP Transformation), with iterative summaries that evolve as solutions emerge (p. 44-48).
A "Catchball" approach involves the Board, Networks, and Clinical Teams in developing annual priorities, ensuring alignment between strategy and operations (p. 6).
Population health intelligence tools (e.g., HARRl) are used to engage communities and target interventions at priority wards, linking data directly to action (p. 37).
The Trust actively participates in the Lancashire and South Cumbria Provider Collaborative to drive system-wide standardisation and efficiency (p. 26).
Digital inclusion is specifically addressed, with plans to mitigate digital exclusion when accessing services (p. 15).
The Estates enabler plan includes developing "Community Roots" neighbourhood centres and Mental Health Emergency Departments (MHEDs) funded by national strategic capital (p. 30-32).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
p. 7-8: The health inequalities plan embeds "prevention" as a core principle, using population health intelligence to enable "proactive, preventative approaches" and targeting upstream interventions.
p. 12: The plan to reduce Adult Acute Length of Stay is driven by a "left-shift investment in community services, particularly Assertive Engagement functions... and through strengthened neighbourhood and Place partnerships," enabling earlier intervention and preventing admissions.
analysis.SUMMARY: Covers LSC area with data-driven population health targeting. | Mapped to 10YHP via Quadruple Aim framework. | Specific improvement plans and trajectories for NOF metrics. | Community investment versus inpatient reduction, with planned staffing reductions. | Population health intelligence for proactive, upstream interventions. | Adoption plans for NHS App, FDP, EPR, and AI. | Strengthening neighbourhood hubs and place-based care models. | Joy at work, sickness reduction, and digital upskilling focus. | Equity embedded in planning with community co-design. | LSCI improvement method and system-wide pathway redesign. | Rolling plan, risk strategy, and iterative A3s.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
p. 4, Executive Summary: Describes a "virtuous circle of increasing community investment and reducing reliance on inpatient care,"  linking resource shift to the "hospital to community" shift.
p. 21-22, Table 5: The Efficiency Improvement Programme (EIP) for 2026/27 totals £17.7m, with a risk-based assessment of schemes. It notes that where schemes imply staffing reductions, exit costs may be a "call on the Trust's ambitions," indicating a trade-off between savings and one-off costs.
p. 22, Table 6: Shows a planned £20.5m total reduction in bank and agency spend over three years, shifting resources from temporary to substantive staffing or other areas.
analysis.VISION: "To provide the best mental health, learning disability, autism and community‑based services for the populations we serve."	p. 5
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
p. 5, 16: The Quadruple Aim includes "Joy and Pride in Work." The Workforce Strategic Vision (p. 16) states colleagues "are our most valuable asset" and aims for them to "feel valued, experience joy and feel pride."
p. 16: A detailed "colleague attendance improvement plan" addresses high sickness rates through prevention, leadership, evidence-based approaches, digital tools, and grip and control.
p. 17: Describes a collaborative approach to workforce planning involving transformation, professional leads, recruitment, PMO, and clinical networks.
