analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Three future care models aligned to 10 Year Health Plan shifts: hospital to community, analogue to digital, treatment to prevention (p. 6, 50)—Acute and Specialist Hospital Care, Neighbourhood & City‑wide Community Care, and Diagnosis & Treatment Planning. 

Collectively, the NMPP Lung Health project demonstrates how collaboration across providers and places can shift the system from reactive treatment towards prevention, equity and long‑term population health improvement, while making more effective use of specialist expertise and community capacity. It provides a practical blueprint for place‑based, integrated respiratory care that improves outcomes for children, adults and families, particularly in communities experiencing the greatest health inequalities. (p.92)
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Phased trajectories: Referral to Treatment (RTT) to 92%, Accident & Emergency (A&E) to 85%, diagnostics to 1% by 2028/29 (p. 30-31). Delivery dependent on system-wide action on flow, discharge, and neighbourhood services. Financial non-compliance for LUHFT/LWH due to structural deficit funding withdrawal (p. 8-9, 40).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Group Transformation Plan (GTP) with annual refresh (p. 69). Board Assurance Framework (BAF) with 10 strategic risks, regularly reviewed (p. 81-85). Financial Recovery Group weekly oversight (p. 41). Phased corporate services implementation (p. 90).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
Neighbourhood and City-wide Community Care model  defined (p. 52-53). North Mersey Provider Partnership (NMPP) with Alder Hey and Mersey Care to integrate community services (p. 91-92). Virtual wards, remote monitoring, admission avoidance programmes (p. 7-8).
"Hospital care forms the majority of what we do within the UHL Group and LAASP, but our member trusts are also providers of a significant amount of community-based care. Some of our services have been moving care into the community for a number of years. Often this has been activity like routine outpatient follow-up appointments, but even this has sometimes been met with challenges." (p.53)
DELIVERY: • Liverpool Heart and Chest Hospital (LHCH) RTT: ahead of plan.
• Further reductions as corporate service models implemented (p. 90).
• Corporate Services TUPE transfer of 578 WTE into LUHFT (p. 90).
• Continued focus on sickness absence reduction.
Financial sustainability plan addressing structural deficits.
• LUHFT/LWH plans non-compliant due to structural deficit funding withdrawal (p. 8-9, 40).
Data strategy fully embedded; AI-enabled pathways.
• Clinical pathway redesign (cardiology, stroke, women's health) (p. 24-25, 51).
• Diagnosis and Treatment Planning models scaled.
• North Mersey Provider Partnership (NMPP) Lung Health project (p. 92-93).
Fully integrated health and academic campus.
• Health Innovation Liverpool – Health Science Campus development (p. 93-94).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Single Electronic Patient Record (EPR) across five trusts as cornerstone (p. 79). Federated Data Platform (FDP), ambient voice technology, artificial intelligence (AI), automation, NHS App, remote monitoring. Data strategy to support prevention, research, and innovation (p. 79-80).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Prevention and Health Inequalities Group (PHIG) with three-phase plan (p. 18). Targeted action in Knowsley, Liverpool, Sefton using Combined Intelligence for Population Health Action (CIPHA) data (p. 17-18). CORE20PLUS5 populations  considered in pathway redesign and access (p. 18).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Health Innovation Liverpool – Health Science Campus with University of Liverpool (p. 93-94). Liverpool Neonatal Partnership (p. 27). Research Strategy aiming for top UK research-active status (p. 76-77). Innovation Strategy for commercial partnerships and AI adoption (p. 78).
OTHER: P.6 - three future care models—Acute and Specialist Hospital Care, Neighbourhood & City‑wide Community Care, and Diagnosis & Treatment Planning—which together form the organising framework for our transformation over the medium term.
North Mersey Provider Partnership (NMPP) Lung Health project addresses respiratory health across cradle-to-grave pathway, tackling wider determinants (p. 92-93).
Health Innovation Liverpool – Health Science Campus with University of Liverpool transforms former Royal Liverpool Hospital site into research and education hub (p. 93-94).
Liverpool Neonatal Partnership integrates neonatal and surgical care across Alder Hey and Liverpool Women's Hospital (p. 27).
Group Transformation Plan (GTP) provides portfolio-led framework with clear accountability, milestones, and benefits tracking (p. 69).
Prevention and Health Inequalities Group (PHIG) coordinates anchor institution work across tobacco, alcohol, and healthy weight (p. 18).
Board Assurance Framework (BAF) tracks 10 strategic risks with regular review at Assurance and Risk Committee (ARC) (p. 81-85).
Outpatient transformation analysis of 134,000 first appointments identified opportunity to redesign pathways (p. 55).
Life course statistics infographic (p.16)
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Prevention and Health Inequalities Group (PHIG) established to coordinate action (p. 18). Anchor institution role: tobacco dependency, alcohol care, healthy weight programmes. North Mersey Provider Partnership (NMPP) Lung Health project addresses wider determinants (housing, air quality, smoking) (p. 92-93).
analysis.SUMMARY: Serves over 2.5 million across Cheshire and Merseyside. North Mersey population with high deprivation and poor health literacy. | Three care models  mirror national hospital-to-community shift. | Transformation plan credible, but financial non-compliance undermines shift. | Major workforce reduction to fund EPR and corporate consolidation. | Anchor institution programmes targeting tobacco, alcohol, and healthy weight. | Single EPR across five trusts as foundational transformation enabler. | NMPP partnership driving integrated community pathways. | Corporate consolidation enables flexible careers across group. | PHIG coordinates data-driven action on CORE20PLUS5 populations. | Health Innovation Campus and Neonatal Partnership exemplars. | Group Transformation Plan with annual refresh and BAF oversight.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Workforce reduction of 2,364 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) over three years (p. 43) to deliver £113.3m Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) (p. 43). Corporate services consolidation transfers 578 WTE into LUHFT, enabling efficiency (p. 90).
analysis.VISION:
Our future:
Acute and specialist hospital care - Our hospitals and specialists doing what only a hospital can do
Diagnosis & treatment planning - Services and pathways that give patients a rapid diagnosis and plan for their further care
Neighbourhood and city-wide community care - Working with our
NHS partners to provide care closer to home (p.49)

The ambition is for the UHL Group to become one of the UK’s leading research‑active NHS organisations (p.76)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Corporate Services Programme consolidating functions (p. 90-91). Focus on leadership, talent development, flexible careers across group. Response to generational labour market changes, AI, and automation (p. 62-63). Staff networks, wellbeing, anchor institution employment practices (p. 64).

analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Workforce reduction of 2,364 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) over three years (p. 43) to deliver £113.3m Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) (p. 43). Corporate services consolidation transfers 578 WTE into LUHFT, enabling efficiency (p. 90).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Organisational strategy refreshed to align with 10 Year Health Plan three shifts: hospital to community, analogue to digital, sickness to prevention (p. 3). Aligned to Greater Manchester Strategy 2025-2035, ICB strategic commissioning plan, and emerging Neighbourhood Health Plans for Manchester and Trafford (p. 3-4).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Digital shift well-advanced with Hive Electronic Patient Record (EPR) (Epic) and plans to extend to community services (p. 32). Hospital to community shift driven by Local Care Organisations (LCOs), Integrated Neighbourhood Teams (INTs), and 'Care Closer to Home' programme (p. 8, 28-29). Prevention embedded through GM 'Live Well' programme, public health interventions, and population health focus (p. 5, 36-37). All constitutional standards have compliant trajectories to 2028/29 (p. 7).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) with strategic risks monitored by Board committees (p. 45-48). Comprehensive risk assessment for plan delivery with mitigations (p. 45-48). Delivery Oversight Framework with quarterly Clinical Group reviews and monthly performance reviews (p. 49-50). Integrated Performance Report (IPR) with balanced scorecard (p. 49). Quality Impact Assessments (QIA) and Equality and Health Inequality Impact Assessments (EHIA) for all major changes (p. 45, 52).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 3). Priorities include: Local Care Organisations (LCOs) in Manchester and Trafford, Integrated Neighbourhood Teams (INTs), 'Care Closer to Home' programme, 'Hospital@Home' expansion, Community Diagnostic Centre (CDC) development, Tier 2 community-based services (Gynaecology, Ear, Nose and Throat (ENT), Diabetes) (p. 8, 28-29, 33-34).
DELIVERY: • Close 88 beds already; aim to close c.100 beds (p. 28).
• Value for Patients (VfP) cumulative target £175m over three years (p. 25, 42).
• Extend Hive Electronic Patient Record (EPR) to community services (p. 32).
• MyMFT fully aligned with NHS App (p. 32).
• Mature left-shift with reduced acute demand.
• Community Diagnostic Centre (CDC) bid for Gorton (p. 28).
• Every patient with major health condition has research study available (p. 35).
• Automated trial eligibility prompts in Electronic Patient Record (EPR) (p. 35).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Comprehensive digital programme: Hive Electronic Patient Record (EPR) (Epic), community EPR extension, Federated Data Platform (FDP), Ambient Voice Technology, Artificial Intelligence (AI) Academy, predictive analytics, MyMFT patient portal aligned with NHS App, data dashboards (Power BI), Genomic Medicine development (p. 31-32, 36).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Comprehensive Equality and Health Inequality Impact Assessment (EHIA) completed for the plan (p. 52-87). Detailed analysis of impacts across all protected characteristics and health inequality groups. Positive impacts identified for looked after children, carers, homeless people, people involved in criminal justice system, people with addictions, people on low income, people with poor literacy, people in deprived areas, refugees and asylum seekers (p. 67-79). Specific actions include: refreshed homelessness policy, hospital navigator service, carers passport, health literacy improvements, poverty mitigation, neighbourhood health plans targeting deprivation (p. 69-78).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Research and innovation as core strategic aim (p. 5). Key innovations: Innovative Technology Adoption Programme (iTAP), strategic partnerships with pharmaceutical and life sciences companies, automated trial eligibility prompts in Electronic Patient Record (EPR), Artificial Intelligence (AI) Academy, Genomic Medicine, Brain Health service for dementia, robotic surgery, digital pathology, wearables (p. 34-36). North Manchester General Hospital (NMGH) New Hospital Programme (Wave One) (p. 43).
OTHER: Hive Electronic Patient Record (EPR) (Epic) successfully implemented, with plans to extend to community services (p. 31-32).
North Manchester General Hospital (NMGH) New Hospital Programme – Wave One scheme with £1bn-£1.5bn budget, due on site 2029 (p. 43).
Innovative Technology Adoption Programme (iTAP) to streamline introduction of innovative solutions (p. 35).
Value for Patients (VfP) programme with cumulative £175m savings target over three years (p. 25, 42).
Artificial Intelligence (AI) Academy to ensure safe, clinically led adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI) (p. 32).
Genomic Medicine mainstreaming, building on position as national leader (p. 6).
Brain Health service to change how dementia is diagnosed and managed (p. 57).
Local Care Organisations (LCOs) in Manchester and Trafford delivering integrated community care (p. 11, 33).
Comprehensive Equality and Health Inequality Impact Assessment (EHIA) with detailed analysis and actions across all protected characteristics and health inequality groups (p. 52-87).
Delivery Oversight Framework with quarterly Clinical Group reviews, monthly performance reviews, and Integrated Performance Report (IPR) (p. 49-50).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: "Sickness to prevention" is a core shift (p. 3). Priorities include: GM 'Live Well' programme support, increased public health interventions, population health focus, targeted neighbourhood-based health interventions (health champions, vaccinations, screening, 'Healthy Hearts'), and partnership with University of Manchester for clinical placements (p. 5, 36-37).
analysis.SUMMARY: Two localities; stark health divide between Manchester and Trafford. | Five aims explicitly mapped to three national shifts. | Hive EPR and LCOs provide foundation; scale and partnerships critical. | £175m savings over three years; capital prioritisation essential. | GM Live Well; public health interventions; population health focus. | Hive EPR live; community EPR, AI academy, and FDP planned. | LCOs and INTs driving neighbourhood care; Hospital@Home expanding. | Clinically-led model; agency to zero by 2029; digital skills academy. | Comprehensive EHIA with targeted actions for all inequality groups. | iTAP; NMGH rebuild; genomics; Brain Health service. | Delivery Oversight Framework; BAF; EHIA for all changes.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Value for Patients (VfP) programme requires £175m cumulative savings over three years (p. 25, 42). Agency spend to be eliminated by 2029 (p. 7, 39). Bank spend reduced by 10% year-on-year (p. 7). Capital prioritisation process ensures maximum benefit from constrained allocation (p. 47). Bids for regional and national transformation funds to support estates and digital projects (p. 47).
analysis.VISION:
Our Vision: 
A thriving city region where everyone can live a good life

Our Priority System Outcomes	
To improve Healthy Life Expectancy in GM so that it at least matches the NW of England by 2030
To close the gap in HLE between the most and least advantaged in GM in line with the 10- year ambition to at least halve that gap by 2035 
Raise the heathiest generation of children and young people (10 Year Heith Plan ambition) Detailed metric in development

Our Missions
Strengthen our communities
Help people stay well and detect illness earlier
Recover core NHS and care services
Support our workforce and our carers
Help people get into - and stay in - good work
Achieve financial sustainability

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan is built around the three shifts: hospital to community, sickness to prevention, analogue to digital (p. 3, 6-7). Aligned to Cheshire and Merseyside ICB's strategic commissioning plan and Joint Forward Plan (p. 118-119). The Trust's five life-course Models of Care directly translate national ambition into local delivery logic (p. 26-27).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
The plan deliberately front-loads financial and workforce recovery to create headroom for transformation (p. 8, 10): "The Board has taken a deliberate decision to pursue a higher-risk delivery approach in the early years of the IDTP... This approach reflects a conscious acceptance of elevated delivery, workforce and regulatory risk in the short term, rather than deferring risk or embedding longer-term structural fragility."

Referral to Treatment (RTT) 92% and cancer standards are delivered by Year 3 (p. 112). Urgent and Emergency Care (UEC) is stabilised at 78% by Year 1, with improvement towards 85% set as direction of travel beyond Year 3 (p. 10, 112). Neighbourhood health is scaled selectively in Years 2-3, with full maturity beyond the plan (p. 60). The shift is therefore achieved in stages, with delivery confidence explicitly moderated.
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: The plan is explicitly designed as a "living medium-term plan" with formal refresh points (p. 13, 28). Appendices define boundaries, deferrals, and refresh triggers (Appendix F, p. 106-110). Delivery confidence is explicitly rated and linked to re-phasing (p. 96-104). Governance through the Executive Integrated Transformation Group (EITG) and Board provides oversight and course correction (p. 74, 80-81).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 3). The Trust's role as a neighbourhood integrator is defined (p. 30). Priorities include: virtual wards, hospital-at-home, integrated neighbourhood teams, improved intermediate care and discharge pathways, and community-based diagnostics (Crewe Diagnostic Hub) (p. 31-32, 60). Delivery is phased, with selective scaling in Years 2-3 based on evidence and partner readiness (p. 60).
DELIVERY: • Priority on flow, discharge, safety-critical pathways (p. 35-36).
• ≤5.0% waiting over six weeks by March 2027 (p. 113).
Workforce cost reduction sustained. Conditional on redesign and delivery confidence.
• Deficit: £1.3m (Y4); Breakeven by end of Year 5 (p. 111).
• Front-loaded recovery with material delivery risk (p. 52).
• Transition point to sustainable financial performance (p. 53).
• Strengthen interfaces with primary care, community, social care (p. 59-60).
• Selective scaling of neighbourhood models where evidence supports impact (p. 31, 60).
• Full neighbourhood model maturity extends beyond IDTP.
• Frailty, discharge, and intermediate care priorities (p. 31, 40).
• Diagnostic Hub in Crewe (subject to approval) (p. 60).
• Stabilise and embed existing digital tools in priority pathways (p. 57).
• Enable pathway redesign and workforce productivity (p. 58).
• Further enable pathway redesign and workforce productivity (p. 58).
• Advanced use of data and digital.
• Embed digital tools across more pathways (p. 58).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Digital enablement is a key enabler (p. 56-58). Priorities include: real-time visibility of patient status and flow, digitally supported diagnostics ordering and results management, electronic documentation, pathway-level performance insight, and adoption of the Federated Data Platform (p. 57-58). Digital is sequenced to support stabilisation (Year 1), scaling (Years 2-3), and advanced use (Years 4-5) (p. 57-58).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
A dedicated appendix (Appendix J) assesses equity implications of sequencing choices (p. 122-127). Prioritised cohorts (frailty, multiple long-term conditions, children with additional needs) align with groups facing inequity (p. 19). Mitigations include targeted cohort focus, assisted access, and monitoring of variation (p. 125-126). Core20PLUS5 is referenced in the context of children and young people (p. 12).

Champion belonging, inclusion and opportunity: Increase diversity in our leadership roles (Bands 6–9), deliver the Anti-Racism Framework to Gold standard by 2030, and achieve a top-quartile NHS Staff Survey “Inclusion” sub-score by 2036 (p.91)
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: The "Healthier Futures" transformation programme is the primary vehicle for innovation (p. 38-42). Key innovations include: partnership with The Christie for oncology (p. 24, 40), the new Leighton Hospital as a catalyst for redesign (p. 7), the proposed Crewe Diagnostic Hub (p. 60), and the Research, Training and Development Centre (subject to approval) (p. 59).
OTHER: "1-3-5 planning approach" differentiates high-confidence delivery (Year 1), conditional plans (Years 2-3), and direction of travel (Years 4-5) (p. 6).
Front-loaded risk acceptance – the Board deliberately accepted higher short-term delivery risk to stabilise services and avoid long-term fragility (p. 8).
Healthier Futures transformation programme provides a single, integrated structure for pathway redesign, operating model change, and productivity (p. 38).
Alliance Partnership with neighbouring trusts aims to improve clinical and workforce resilience, shared pathway design, and efficiency at scale (p. 70-71).
Quality and Equality Impact Assessment (QEIA) is embedded at plan level, with explicit consideration of sequencing impacts on protected groups (Appendix K, p. 128-134).
Equity implications of sequencing are assessed in Appendix J, with mitigations for groups at risk of delayed benefit (p. 122-127).
Refreshed values (Compassion, Courage, Commitment, Community) underpin cultural and behavioural change (p. 19).
New Hospital Programme (NHP) is treated as a catalyst for redesign, not just an estate replacement, with organisational readiness a key IDTP commitment (p. 7, 58-59).
Partnership with The Christie for oncology services aims to improve sustainability, access, and workforce resilience (p. 24, 40).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
"Sickness to prevention" is a core shift (p. 3). Priorities include: neighbourhood-based multidisciplinary care, anticipatory support for frail and complex patients, virtual wards, hospital-at-home, and integrated care for people with multiple long-term conditions (p. 22-23, 31). The "Help Me Stay Well" Model of Care is explicitly prevention-focused (p. 26-27).

Priorities:
•Neighbourhood-based multidisciplinary care and anticipatory support, particularly for people living with frailty, long-term conditions and complex needs, delivered in partnership with primary care, local authorities and the voluntary sector.
•Virtual wards and hospital-at-home models, selectively scaled where evidence demonstrates impact on admissions, length of stay, patient experience and safety.
•Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways, reducing avoidable admissions and improving urgent and emergency care flow.
•Redesigned elective pathways, including pre-operative optimisation, straight-to-test diagnostics and one-stop models, supporting elective recovery and productivity.
•Improved diagnostic pathways and turnaround, as a critical enabler of elective, cancer and urgent care performance.
•Cancer pathway redesign, including faster diagnostics and improved pathway coordination, to support recovery against national standards.
•Reliable discharge and transfer of care models, including criteria-led discharge and strengthened interfaces with community and neighbourhood services.
•Digitally enabled care coordination, using core EPR functionality to support safer clinical decision-making, flow and productivity.
analysis.SUMMARY: Mid Cheshire; priority cohorts from patient-level segmentation. | Models of Care directly translate three shifts locally. | Front-loaded recovery creates headroom; success depends on delivery capacity and system flow. | 6% Year 1 CIP and 707 WTE reduction to stabilise services. | Help Me Stay Well model targets frailty and long-term conditions. | Phased adoption to stabilise, enable redesign, then advance. | Phased neighbourhood scaling based on evidence, not uniform rollout. | Supporting people through necessary redesign, not just headcount reduction. | Equity risks from sequencing explicitly assessed and mitigated. | Healthier Futures programme; Alliance and Christie partnerships. | Living plan with explicit refresh triggers and re-phasing.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: A front-loaded Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) of 6% in Year 1 (p. 52) and a workforce reduction of c. 707 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) over three years (p. 10, 43) are accepted to create financial headroom and stabilise services. This represents a deliberate decision to accept higher short-term risk to avoid embedding longer-term structural fragility (p. 8). Up to £3m of targeted investment is available for critical quality and safety risks, but is tightly governed (p. 52).
analysis.VISION:
"A healthier future for our communities by delivering high-quality, connected care through neighbourhoods and Place-based working." p. 7

“We provide high-quality, connected care across hospital, community and neighbourhood settings — creating a healthier future for the people of Cheshire through prevention, personalisation and partnership.”
This vision is grounded in a life-course and neighbourhood approach, focusing on earlier intervention, better continuity and local support, alongside safe and productive hospital services. (p.20)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
Workforce is treated as the primary constraint (p. 17, 43). The plan prioritises supporting colleagues through change, with a focus on retention, wellbeing, and safety (p. 45). Leadership, engagement, and capability are strengthened through the "Thriving Culture" programme, values, and leadership development (p. 48-49). Productivity is framed as an outcome of redesign, not intensification (p. 48).
Productivity improvement within the IDTP is expected to arise primarily from deliberate service redesign, improved flow and digitally enabled ways of working. It is not assumed to come from incremental efficiency savings or from asking staff to work harder within existing models of care. (p.48).

Leadership and culture: Over the IDTP period, the Trust will continue to strengthen:
•Visible clinical and managerial leadership at all levels, aligned to the national Management & Leadership Framework.
•Inclusive and compassionate leadership behaviours, supporting psychological safety, belonging and engagement.
•Leadership capability for digital, neighbourhood-based and cross-boundary working.
•Meaningful engagement with colleagues and trade unions, particularly where roles, rotas or ways of working change.
•Practical improvement capability and support for teams leading change, especially in high-pressure services.
Together, these actions underpin retention,

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
p. 2, Executive Summary:  states the plan is "aligned to national priorities, including the NHS 10-Year Plan and the three strategic shifts towards prevention, care closer to home and greater use of digital and data."
p. 6, sec 2.4: Confirms the Trust Strategy has been "developed with explicit regard to the NHS 10-Year Health Plan, including the three strategic shifts."
p. 10, sec 3.3: Describes lead commissioning arrangement with Lancashire and South Cumbria ICB and close working with other ICBs, demonstrating alignment with system planning.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
p. 14, sec 4.4: Provides a phased trajectory for C2 response times, with specific targets: 24:58 in 26/27, improving to 22-23 minutes in 27/28, and 21-22 minutes in 28/29.
p. 14: Acknowledges that achieving the national ambition of 18 minutes by 2028/29 "remains highly challenging given the scale of the Trust and the degree of system change required," showing realistic assessment.
p. 18, sec 5.6: Sets out phased workforce delivery, with growth in Years 1-3 and consolidation/integration in Years 4-5.
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
p. 2, Executive Summary: States that the plan enables "priorities and assumptions to be reviewed, refined and re-sequenced in response to delivery progress and system conditions."
p. 11, sec 3.5: Delivery capacity "is kept under review throughout the year through established governance and assurance arrangements. Where pressures emerge, priorities may be re-sequenced through the annual planning and assurance process."
p. 14, sec 4.5: Acknowledges that "assumptions within the medium-term plan are cautious" regarding hospital handover performance, and "further modelling for Years 2 and 3 will continue as part of the final submission process and will be refreshed annually."
p. 25, sec 8.2: States that during annual planning, "delivery priorities are tested for achievability... Where constraints are identified, priorities may be re-phased or adjusted to maintain grip on delivery and sustainability."
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
p. 2, Executive Summary:  aligned to the shift "towards prevention, care closer to home."
p. 7, sec 2.6: Aims to "increase hear and treat, reduce conveyance and ensure patients receive care in the clinically most appropriate place."
p. 13, sec 4.3: Clinical Response Strategic Plan focuses on "delivering a resilient, efficient and patient-centred response model... reducing avoidable conveyance and handover delays."
p. 18, sec 5.5: Links directly to 10YHP: "Hospital to community: Workforce development supports reduced conveyance, increased see and treat, and enhanced clinical navigation through integrated contact centre models, enabling care closer to home."
p. 23, sec 7.3: System collaboration focuses on "improving clinical navigation and reducing avoidable conveyance; supporting integrated models of care that enable treatment closer to home; enabling the shift from hospital to community."
DELIVERY: • Achieve C2 mean response time of 24:58 (p. 14).
• Improve C2 mean to 22-23 minutes range (p. 14).
• Improve C2 mean to 21-22 minutes range (p. 14).
• Phased increase in Double-Crewed Ambulance (DCA) production (p. 14).
• Improve hear and treat, reduce conveyance.
• Grow frontline clinical, call handling, dispatch capacity (p. 17).
• Direct workforce growth aligned to activity increases (p. 18).
• Use third-party provision for flexibility while scaling training (p. 18).
• Increase corporate efficiency via digital and shared services.
• Higher efficiency requirement (2.6%) to address underlying deficit (p. 20).
• Strengthen core digital foundations and cyber security (p. 15).
• Improve data quality and analytics capability.
• Active participation in UEC Boards, provider collaboratives (p. 10).
• Strengthen partnership on alternative pathways and digital referral (p. 7).
• Fully integrated urgent and emergency care system.
• Work with ICBs across footprint (L&SC, GM, C&M).
• Collaborate via Northern Ambulance Alliance and AACE (p. 10).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
p. 7, sec 2.6: Highlights "strategic need to utilise digital enablers to improve both access and response to patients. This will include automation of process, access for patients where English is not their first language and integration of our core operating platforms."
p. 13, sec 4.3: Clinical Response Strategic Plan includes "delivering an integrated, technology-enabled contact model."
p. 15, sec 4.6: Digital priorities focus on "maintaining and strengthening core digital foundations," "improving the utilisation of data," and "delivering a digital shift by accelerating transformation and innovation."
p. 18, sec 5.5: Workforce plans reflect "continued digital development supports productivity, quality and new ways of working."
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
p. 8, sec 2.7: Provides detailed analysis of health inequalities across the footprint, noting "32-34% of the population live in the most deprived 20% of areas nationally," with life expectancy gaps of up to 12 years in parts of Blackpool. It  links deprivation to demand: "the most deprived 20% of the population accounts for approximately 28% of face-to-face emergency incidents."
p. 11, sec 3.4: States that "population health analysis and health inequality insights are drawn from local authority and Integrated Care System health intelligence... used to inform delivery actions focused on reducing inequalities, supporting prevention and improving equity of access."
p. 13, sec 4.2: Quality priorities include "improving the effectiveness of care, with a specific focus on addressing health inequalities through delivery of the Trust's Health Inequalities Framework."
p. 28, sec 9.3: Confirms that Equality Impact Assessments have been completed for key elements of the plan, with the Board assured that "quality and equality impacts have been appropriately identified, considered and addressed."
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
p. 7, sec 2.6: Identifies "digital enablers to improve both access and response... including automation of process, access for patients where English is not their first language and integration of our core operating platforms."
p. 13, sec 4.3: Clinical Response Strategic Plan includes "integrated, technology-enabled contact model" and "improving resource deployment, reducing avoidable conveyance and handover delays."
p. 22-23, sec 7.1-7.4: Improvement-led transformation approach, aligned to NHS IMPACT framework, is positioned as the primary mechanism for delivering transformation. This includes embedding improvement across the organisation, building capability, and implementing an "improvement-focused management system."
p. 23, sec 7.3: System collaboration focuses on "improving clinical navigation and reducing avoidable conveyance; supporting integrated models of care that enable treatment closer to home; and enabling the shift from hospital to community."
OTHER: OPTIMA modelling is used to identify productivity opportunities and inform capacity planning (p. 11).
NHS IMPACT framework underpins the improvement-led transformation approach, embedding continuous improvement across the organisation (p. 22).
Northern Ambulance Alliance and AACE membership enables cross-service collaboration and shared best practice (p. 10).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
p. 2, Executive Summary: The plan is  aligned to the "shift towards prevention."
p. 7, sec 2.6: Identifies the need to "work with our system partners to maximise, coordinate and design alternative pathways of care. This will increase hear and treat, reduce conveyance and ensure patients receive care in the clinically most appropriate place."
p. 13, sec 4.3: Clinical Response Strategic Plan includes "improving clinical navigation and reducing avoidable conveyance" and "strengthening integration with system partners across urgent and emergency care pathways."
analysis.SUMMARY: Covers 7m population with detailed deprivation analysis and demand links | aligned to three shifts and ICB commissioning arrangements | Phased C2 targets with realistic assessment of 18-minute challenge | Short-term agency use for long-term substantive workforce growth | Focus on hear and treat, reducing conveyance, alternative pathways | Prioritises automation, data utilisation, and integrated contact model | Reduces conveyance through navigation and system pathways | Improvement-led culture with empowered frontline and wellbeing focus | Deprivation data informs action via Health Inequalities Framework | NHS IMPACT improvement method as primary transformation driver | Annual refresh with priorities re-sequenced based on delivery progress
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
p. 18, sec 5.6: Describes a clear resource shift: "use of third-party provision in Year 1 to provide flexibility while training capacity is scaled; and direct workforce growth in Years 2 and 3." This shows a trade-off between short-term flexibility and long-term substantive growth.
p. 18: Also notes "increased opportunities for corporate efficiency, driven by digital solutions, shared services and revised operating models" in Years 4-5, indicating a shift from growth to efficiency.
p. 20, sec 6.1: Financial plans embed a "higher efficiency requirement in 2026/27 to address the Trust's underlying deficit," demonstrating a trade-off between cost reduction and investment.
analysis.VISION: To provide outstanding, inclusive care for everyone we serve, delivered through a safe, supportive culture and responsive partnerships, underpinned by continuous improvement and innovation.	p. 2
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
p. 7, sec 2.6: Identifies need to "embed improvement methodology and enhance improvement capability with operational delivery."
p. 16-17, sec 5.1-5.2: People and Culture Strategic Plan focuses on five objectives: attract and recruit a representative workforce; develop confident and inclusive leaders; provide transparent career pathways; build an inclusive, supportive and safe culture; embed a listening culture.
p. 18, sec 5.7: Describes wellbeing and culture initiatives including "comprehensive wellbeing offer, including occupational health, mental health support, TRiM, leadership development and a Wellbeing Hub."
p. 22, sec 7.1-7.2: Improvement-led transformation approach positions improvement as "the Trust's primary mechanism for delivering transformation and productivity," with improvement "led by empowered frontline staff, supported by leaders with the skills and tools to manage complexity."

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Aligned to the three shifts: analogue to digital, hospital to community, treatment to prevention (p. 35). Connects with Greater Manchester (GM) Integrated Care System (ICS) strategic direction and GM's 5 Year Strategic Commissioning Plan (p. 5, 31-32). Clinical Strategy principles draw directly on national and regional ambitions (p. 29-30).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Phased trajectory aims for financial balance by 2028-29 and constitutional standards (p. 4, 8-9, 36). Delivery of digital shift is contingent on securing match funding for a single Electronic Patient Record (EPR) – a critical dependency (p. 4, 7, 46). Hospital to community shift demonstrated through Hospital at Home expansion and neighbourhood work in Rochdale (p. 24, 30, 32). Prevention shift embedded through Population Health and Health Inequalities programme (p. 27).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) with key risks reviewed regularly (p. 41-42). Strengthened risk governance with Executive Risk Oversight Group (EROG) and Operational Risk Management Group (ORMG) (p. 41, 44). Speciality review framework will identify opportunities and validate plans (p. 18, 30). Financial plan includes "best case", "most likely case", and "worst case" assessments (p. 17-18).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Neighbourhood health is a key principle of the Clinical Leadership Model (CLM) (p. 31). NCA is part of the National Neighbourhood Health Improvement collaborative in Rochdale (p. 30, 32). Expansion of Hospital at Home and virtual wards enables care at home (p. 24, 30). Strong partnership with localities (Bury, Oldham, Rochdale, Salford) to design neighbourhood services (p. 31).
DELIVERY: • Planned deficit: £57.9m (excluding Deficit Support Funding of £28.8m) (p. 36).
• Cost Improvement Programme (CIP): 5.8% recurrent (£111.6m total target) (p. 36).
• Extend savings into years 2 and 3 through system-wide efficiencies and enabling schemes (e.g., Electronic Patient Record (EPR)) (p. 36).
• Improve RTT performance from 54.2% (Oct 25) towards national milestones (p. 12).
• Scale up outpatient initiatives (Patient Initiated Follow Up (PIFU), Advice and Guidance, Did Not Attend (DNA) reduction) (p. 22-23).
• Expand Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) and Hospital at Home.
• Implement Urgent Treatment Centre at Royal Oldham Hospital (ROH) (p. 24).
• Improve 28-day Faster Diagnosis Standard (FDS) from 74.2% (Oct 25) towards 80% (p. 14).
• Maintain clinically led approach through Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) (p. 6, 23).
• Reduce sickness absence and agency spend (p. 6-7, 34).
• Implement wellbeing and Speak Up initiatives.
• Progress EPR business case and readiness (data cleansing, application rationalisation) (p. 27-28, 37-38).
Digital maturity; AI and automation embedded.
• Continue Electronic Document Management System (EDMS) programme.
• Review of facilities services and contracts (p. 38-39).
• Speciality review framework to validate 2026-27 plans and identify transformational opportunities (p. 18, 30).
• Develop service-level strategic plans aligned to Clinical Strategy.
• Implement new service models (e.g., digitally enabled outpatients, expanded Hospital at Home) (p. 30).
• Scope and deliver larger-scale transformational change with GM partners (p. 30, 32).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Single Trust-wide Electronic Patient Record (EPR) is the top priority to replace fragmented systems and unlock productivity (p. 4, 7, 27-28, 37). Digital strategy includes application rationalisation, improved data quality, Artificial Intelligence (AI), automation, digital literacy programme, and Electronic Document Management System (EDMS) (p. 27-28, 37-38).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Core20PLUS5 framework  used to analyse population health and target action (p. 11). Detailed analysis of healthcare inequalities by ethnicity, deprivation, age, and gender (p. 12). Population Health and Health Inequalities (PHHI) programme embeds equity into clinical groups and pathways. Health inequality objectives included in consultant job planning (p. 27). Equality Impact Assessments (EIAs) mandatory for all changes (p. 48).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Single Electronic Patient Record (EPR) as foundational transformation (p. 4, 27-28). Artificial Intelligence (AI) in chest X-ray and prostate cancer detection (p. 28). Surgical robots in urology, colorectal, gynaecology, and general surgery (p. 28). Greater Manchester (GM) Procurement Service hosted by NCA (p. 32). Speciality review framework to identify transformational opportunities (p. 18, 30).
OTHER: 5 year culture plan (p.40)
Clinical Leadership Model (CLM) places clinical decision-making at the heart of service design and delivery (p. 5, 30-31).
Speciality review framework will identify transformational opportunities and validate annual plans (p. 18, 30).
Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete (RAAC) eradication programme is one of the largest in the NHS, with significant estate transformation potential (p. 8, 15, 39).
Greater Manchester (GM) Procurement Service hosted by NCA will drive non-pay efficiencies across the region (p. 32).
National Neighbourhood Health Improvement collaborative in Rochdale pilots future neighbourhood models (p. 30, 32).
Digital literacy programme and Data Quality Academy build workforce capability for digital shift (p. 28, 38).
Observe, Listen and Act patient feedback model has national acclaim (p. 26, 51).
Four Localities Partnership (FLP) addresses common challenges across Bury, Oldham, Rochdale, and Salford (p. 31).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Population Health and Health Inequalities (PHHI) programme provides strategic direction for prevention, aligned with Clinical Leadership Model (CLM) (p. 27). Prevention embedded within clinical groups and pathways. Existing interventions include smoking cessation, alcohol care teams, vaccination, lifestyle medicine, and social prescribing (p. 27).
analysis.SUMMARY: One million people across four localities with stark inequalities. | Clinical strategy  built around three national shifts. | Digital and financial dependencies critical; neighbourhood work promising but early stage. | EPR investment essential to unlock productivity and enable left shift. | PHHI programme embedding prevention into clinical groups and pathways. | Single EPR foundational to overcome fragmented legacy systems. | Rochdale neighbourhood pilot and Hospital at Home expansion. | Clinical Leadership Model empowers frontline decision-making and accountability. | Core20PLUS5 framework drives action on access and outcomes. | GM Procurement Service and speciality review framework drive system change. | Strengthened risk governance and speciality reviews enable course correction.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Workforce reduction and productivity gains required to deliver  recurrent Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) (p. 36). Investment in EPR is essential to unlock future efficiencies but requires significant capital (p. 4, 7, 46). Estate rationalisation and improved utilisation will release long-term savings (p. 38-39).
analysis.VISION: MISSION: "Saving Lives, Improving Lives" – to be one of the safest, most effective and financially sustainable organisations in the NHS and the place where people want to work. (p.10)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
Clinical Leadership Model (CLM) places clinical decision-making at the heart, with leadership closer to care (p. 5, 30-31). Workforce strategy aligns roles to the three shifts: prevention, community, and digital (p. 35). Focus on wellbeing, reducing sickness absence, and reducing temporary staffing (p. 6-7, 34-35). Investment in leadership development and talent management (p. 39-40).

Trust People Plan four pillars (p.34):
1. Looking after our people – with quality health and wellbeing support for everyone
2. Belonging in the NHS – with a particular focus on tackling the discrimination that some staff face
3. New ways of working and delivering care – making effective use of the full range of our people’s skills and experience
4. Growing for the future – how we recruit and keep our people, and welcome back colleagues who want to return.

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan explicitly aligned to three shifts: hospital to community, sickness to prevention, analogue to digital (p. 4-5). Aligned to Greater Manchester (GM) mental health and wellbeing strategy 'Doing Mental Health Differently 2024-2029' and locality plans (p. 5). Clinical model grounded in left-shift principles (p. 10).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Hospital to community shift driven by Community Transformation Programme, neighbourhood mental health teams, and bolstered crisis services (p. 10, 23). Prevention embedded through early intervention in psychosis, Mental Health Support Teams, and integrated fund reinvesting in local services (p. 5, 14). Digital shift advanced through joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) procurement with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH), electronic prescribing and medicines administration (ePMA), and e-roster (p. 24-25). Zero Out of Area Placements (OAPs) by March 2027 (p. 14).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) with six strategic risks reviewed regularly (p. 28). Comprehensive risk register with mitigations (p. 28). Transformation programmes with Senior Responsible Officer (SRO) accountability and benefits tracking (p. 23-25). Performance management and accountability framework embedded at all levels (p. 29). Quality Impact Assessments (QIA) and Equality Impact Assessments (EIA) for all major changes (p. 31).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 4-5). Priorities include: neighbourhood mental health teams strengthening multi-disciplinary working with primary care and voluntary sector, significant shift to community and crisis services, full implementation of neighbourhood teams, and 'Home First' approach (p. 10, 14, 23).
DELIVERY: Zero 104-week waits for core Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) by March 2027 (p. 14).
Length of Stay (Adult, Psychiatric Intensive Care Unit (PICU), Older Adult)
Reduce average length of stay in line with trajectories agreed by Performance and Finance Committee (p. 14).
Deliver minimum 10% reduction year on year in mental health inpatient care for people with a learning disability and autistic people (p. 15).
• Substantive workforce growth from 4,142 to 4,521 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) by 2029 (p. 16).
• Electronic Prescribing and Medicines Administration (ePMA) rollout (p. 25).
• Investment from Greater Manchester (GM) Integrated Care Board (ICB) expected to continue (p. 19).
• Joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) procurement with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) (p. 24-25).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Joint digital strategy with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) (approved February 2026) (p. 5, 26). Priorities include: joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) procurement, electronic prescribing and medicines administration (ePMA) rollout, e-rostering, patient engagement portal, ambient voice technology, artificial intelligence (AI) (p. 24-26).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Health inequalities steering group chaired by Chief Operating Officer, using Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF) dashboard to monitor progress (p. 7). Annual objectives for care hubs include improving demographic data collection and oversight of length of stay for people with learning disabilities (p. 7). Implementation of Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF) to address racial inequalities, co-designing solutions with racialised communities (p. 18).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Seven transformation programmes with Senior Responsible Officers (SROs): Community, Crisis Model, Inpatient Model, Rehabilitation, Electronic Patient Record (EPR), e-roster, Electronic Prescribing and Medicines Administration (ePMA) (p. 23-25). Integrated fund with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) and Integrated Care Board (ICB) reinvesting savings (p. 14, 19). Joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR) procurement with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) (p. 24-25).
OTHER: Joint digital strategy and Electronic Patient Record (EPR) procurement with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) to align pathways and reduce variation (p. 5, 24-26).
Integrated fund with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) and Integrated Care Board (ICB) reinvests Out of Area Placement (OAP) savings into local services (p. 14, 19).

Mental Health Integrated Provider Network with Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH) and Voluntary, Community, Faith and Social Enterprise (VCFSE) sector to drive change through partnership (p. 8).
'All in it Together' staff ideas scheme to improve productivity (p. 22).
Improvement framework with ABC approach (Aim, Benefit, Change) and structured approach for large-scale change (p. 20-21).
Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF) implementation to address racial inequalities with co-designed solutions (p. 18).
Performance management and accountability framework embedded at all levels, overseen by integrated operational and quality oversight group (p. 14, 29).
Capital bid submitted to repurpose unit for learning disability/autism crisis accommodation as alternative to admission (p. 15).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: "Sickness to prevention" is a core shift (p. 4-5). Priorities include: early intervention in psychosis, Mental Health Support Teams in schools, community transformation bringing services closer to home, integrated offer with Voluntary, Community, Faith and Social Enterprise (VCFSE) partners, and health inequalities steering group with annual priorities for care hubs (p. 5, 7, 23).
analysis.SUMMARY: Five Greater Manchester localities; significant unwarranted variation. | Three ambitions explicitly mirror national shifts; clinical model grounded in left-shift. | Zero OAPs by 2027; neighbourhood teams and joint EPR critical. | Integrated fund reinvests OAP savings into community services. | MHSTs, early intervention, and VCFSE integration. | Joint EPR with GMMH; ePMA and e-roster underway. | Neighbourhood teams and Home First approach. | Anti-racist; bank/agency reduction; peer support roles. | PCREF dashboard and health inequalities steering group. | Integrated fund; joint EPR; seven SRO-led programmes. | BAF, transformation governance, and focus weeks drive improvement.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Underlying deficit of c. £4m managed to breakeven actual position (p. 19). Recurrent breakeven targeted by 2028/29 (p. 19). Integrated fund reinvests savings from reduced Out of Area Placements (OAPs) and spot purchase beds into local services (p. 14, 19). Investment plan supports community and crisis services while reducing inpatient bed base (p. 14, 24).
analysis.VISION:
Our vision: A happier and more hopeful lite for everyone in our communities.
Our values:
Kindness
Fairness
Ingenuity
Determination
Our purpose: Maximise people's potential to live more rewarding lives and create a great place to work. (p.4)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: "Great Place to Work" is a core ambition (p. 4, 11). Workforce plan includes: growth in substantive workforce (4,142 to 4,521 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) by 2029), focus on community and crisis roles, multi-disciplinary teams including therapeutic/psychological roles and peer support, 'Growing our Own' programme, bank and agency reduction, sickness reduction, leadership development, apprenticeship routes, new roles (peer support, therapeutic engagement, nursing associate) (p. 16, 27).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Plan responds to 10 Year Health Plan priorities, aiming for a "significant shift" to seamless care delivered closer to home, and a supportive environment for staff (p. 1). Joint working arrangements designed to create a more resilient, innovative, patient-centred system (p. 1).
−From hospital to community: Emphasis on neighbourhood and preventative care models
−From analogue to digital: Increased use of AI, automation, and digital tools to free up clinical time, with consideration of the Trust Robotic Process Automation (RPA) opportunities.
−From sickness to prevention: Workforce roles will evolve to support proactive, personalised care (p.7)
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Financial breakeven by 2030/31, not within first three years (p.4). Urgent and Emergency Care (UEC) performance remains non-compliant for both Trusts due to bed deficits and discharge challenges (p. 2-3). Delivery of elective targets contingent on additional ICB income (p. 2-3). Digital and estate enablers are foundational but require significant capital (p. 4, 6).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Frameworks (BAF) for both Trusts track strategic risks (financial sustainability, operational delivery, quality of care, workforce, estates and digital) (p. 9-10). Joint Executive Oversight Group for planning (p. 2). Monthly cycle of meetings to monitor key metrics (p. 11). Plan acknowledges material financial risk and need for system support (p. 6).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Vision includes "seamless care, delivered closer to home" (p. 1). Workforce plan references shift "from hospital to community" with emphasis on neighbourhood and preventative care models (p. 7). Stockport has 8 neighbourhoods; Tameside has 4 (p. 1). Single Point of Access (SPOA) and care home conveyance project aim to reduce unnecessary conveyances (p. 3).
DELIVERY: • Tameside & Glossop Integrated Care NHS FT (TGICFT) deficit: £45.5m (p. 5).
• TGICFT deficit: £12.0m (with Deficit Support Funding (DSF) of £12.0m) (p. 5).
• 5% Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) for both Trusts (p. 5).
• Assumed receipt of block contract deconstruction income (p. 6).
• Full receipt of contract funding shortfalls assumed (p. 6).
• 7% Referral to Treatment (RTT) improvement target for both Trusts (p. 2-3).
• Delivery contingent on £3.7m additional income for Stockport; £2.5m for Tameside (p. 2-3).
• Tameside new wards business case (capital dependent) (p. 3).
• Tameside improvement reliant on new wards.
• New wards development at Tameside (subject to capital) (p. 3, 6).
Fully integrated and sustainable service model across both localities.
• Major strategic review of services commenced (p. 1).
• Further alignment of clinical and corporate services.
• Joint Organisational Strategy and enabling strategies in development (p. 1).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Digital priorities include: joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR), expanded patient-facing digital pathways, enhanced interoperability across acute, community, and primary care, upgraded core infrastructure, and use of analytics for population-level planning (p. 4). Robotic Process Automation (RPA) opportunities to be considered (p. 7).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Plan acknowledges "significant health inequalities" in both populations (p. 1). No explicit reference to Core20PLUS5 framework or specific equity interventions.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: "ADOPT" Continuous Improvement Strategic Model with 5 ambitions: Align, Develop, Organisational Partnerships, People at heart, Train (p. 8-9). Transformation priorities include: Emergency Department navigation, Outpatients (Advice & Guidance, Patient Initiated Follow-Up (PIFU)), Cancer Faster Diagnosis Standard, Single Point of Access (SPOA), Inpatient flow (p. 9).
OTHER: New Joint Board operational from April 2026 with extensive corporate governance arrangements (p. 1).
Major strategic review of services to be undertaken during 2026/27, beyond localities (p. 1).

Tameside's bed deficit is the lowest acute bed base in the North West relative to Type 1 Emergency Department activity, acknowledged by Greater Manchester Integrated Care Board (GMICB) and NHS England (NHSE) (p. 3).
Pathology lab replacement at Stockport is a major capital scheme (p. 4, 6).
"ADOPT" Continuous Improvement Model provides a framework for embedding improvement culture (p. 8-9).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Workforce plan references shift "from sickness to prevention" with roles evolving to support proactive, personalised care (p. 7). No specific prevention programmes detailed.
analysis.SUMMARY: SFT (8 neighbourhoods), TGICFT (4), but lacks detailed population data. | Vision  references 10YHP and shifts to seamless care. | Breakeven by year 5, not first three. UEC non-compliant; delivery contingent on uncertain income/enablers. | Relies on Deficit Support Funding, 5% CIP (largely unidentified), and future income assumptions. | Mentioned in workforce plan (sickness to prevention), but no specific programmes. | Joint EPR, expanded digital pathways, enhanced interoperability. | Explicit in vision and workforce plan; SPOA mentioned. | Data-driven planning addresses three shifts; joint People Strategy in development. | Acknowledges health inequalities but no Core20PLUS5 detail. | "ADOPT" CI model; transformation priorities include ED, outpatients, cancer, SPOA. | BAFs track risks; joint executive oversight group.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Both Trusts rely on Deficit Support Funding (DSF) and have been set a 5-year trajectory to breakeven (p. 2, 4). Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) at 5% per year, with a large proportion unidentified for 2026/27 (p. 5). Delivery of targets assumes additional ICB income and block contract deconstruction funding (p. 2-3, 6). Major service reconfiguration required over 3-5 years (p. 6).
analysis.VISION:
Extensive new corporate governance arrangements will be in place from 1 April 2026 that will see a new Joint Board come together alongside the development during 2026/27 of a new joint Organisational Strategy and enabling strategies that include a joint Quality Strategy and a Clinical Strategy.
Our formal collaborative arrangements are focussed on a shared vision for integrated care, designed to create a more resilient, innovative, and patient-centred healthcare system for both populations. (p.1)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Joint People Strategy being refreshed (p. 6). Workforce planning uses five-year retrospective analysis to forecast trends (p. 6-7). Plan addresses three shifts: hospital to community, analogue to digital, sickness to prevention (p. 7). Focus on multidisciplinary teams, digital skills, apprenticeships, and retention (p. 7).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan  aligned to three shifts: treatment to prevention, hospital to community, analogue to digital (p. 4-5). Aligned to Greater Manchester system priorities, the National Cancer Plan for England, and the Medium-Term Planning Framework (p. 5). Neighbourhood oncology programme directly delivers hospital-to-community shift (p. 13).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Prevention shift advanced through neighbourhood oncology, ambulatory acute oncology, and supportive oncology integrated with neighbourhood services (p. 13). Digital shift well-advanced with Future Christie programme (Electronic Patient Record (EPR) by end 2028, Joint Analytics for Cancer (JAC), Artificial Intelligence (AI), Ambient Voice Technology (AVT)) (p. 14-15). Hospital to community shift delivered through neighbourhood oncology and Christie at network expansion (p. 13, 20-21). Delivery of all cancer standards with compliant trajectories (p. 12).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) with strategic risks reviewed regularly by board and assurance committees (p. 23-25). Risk appetite reviewed annually (p. 23). Quality Impact Assessment (QIA) and Equality and Health Impact Assessment (EHIA) for all value improvement schemes (p. 28). Robust governance through audit, quality assurance, and workforce assurance committees (p. 26).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 4). Neighbourhood oncology programme delivers: Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT) in neighbourhoods and at home, ambulatory acute oncology network, supportive oncology integrated with primary care, community and voluntary, community and social enterprise (VCSE) partners (p. 13). Christie at network expansion (p. 8, 20).
DELIVERY: • Reduced unwarranted variation and avoidable bed days (p. 12).
• Expand Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT) in neighbourhoods and at home (p. 13).
• Digital-first pathways using Electronic Patient Record (EPR), NHS App, Christie portal (p. 13).
• Establish pan-Greater Manchester ambulatory acute oncology network (p. 13).
• Single Greater Manchester standards for access and outcomes (p. 13).
• Reduced avoidable Accident & Emergency (A&E) attendances and admissions (p. 13).
• Integrate supportive oncology with neighbourhood services (p. 13).
• Workforce models aligned to neighbourhood delivery (p. 13).
• Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) rollout in clinics and Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDTs) (p. 15).
• JAC dashboards available to clinical and corporate teams (p. 15).
• Artificial Intelligence (AI) in radiotherapy contouring and imaging triage (p. 15).
• Research enablement through JAC (cohort discovery, trial matching) (p. 15).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Plan aligned to three shifts: sickness to prevention, hospital to community, analogue to digital (p. 5, 8). Aligned to Cheshire and Merseyside priorities including addressing backlog, improving access, health inequalities, and preventative strategies (p. 6).
•Sickness to Prevention: Earlier diagnosis, rapid specialist advice, expanded thrombectomy services, and improved chronic neurological disease management to reduce long-term disability and avoidable admissions.
•Hospital to Community: Pathway redesign, expansion of specialist rehabilitation, alternatives to urgent care and hospital attendance and closer integration with community and neighbourhood services.
•Analogue to Digital: Deployment of electronic patient record capability, ambient voice technology, automation, artificial intelligence and shared digital infrastructure across LAASP to improve productivity, staff experience and patient access.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Prevention shift advanced through thrombectomy expansion, rapid access pathways, and chronic disease management (p. 5). Digital shift well-advanced with Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) pilots, single logon, and collaborative Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme (p. 17). Hospital to community shift progressing through pathway redesign, specialist rehabilitation expansion, and alternatives to urgent care (p. 5). Delivery of 92% Referral to Treatment (RTT) by 2028/29 requires significant productivity gains and additional outpatient capacity (p. 16).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Framework (BAF) includes twelve strategic risks reviewed regularly (p. 33). Comprehensive risk register for planning risks (commissioner approach, elective recovery, new payment model, block deconstruction, efficiency delivery) (p. 23, 33). Quality Impact Assessment (QIA) process for all Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) schemes (p. 24). Robust Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) governance with Red/Amber/Green criteria (p. 24-25).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 5, 8). Priorities include: pathway redesign, expansion of specialist rehabilitation, alternatives to urgent care and hospital attendance, closer integration with community and neighbourhood services (p. 5). Spinal Community Assessment Day demonstrated reduced hospital attendances (p. 30-31).
DELIVERY: • Cost Improvement Programme (CIP): £9.1m (4.4% including high-cost drugs) (p. 21, 24).
• Reduce Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infection (CAUTI) rates (p. 11).
• Improve efficiency of pathways (reduce Length of Stay (LOS)) (p. 13).
• Embed Martha's Rule / Call for Concern (p. 12).
• Establish shared HR service across LAASP (p. 18-19).
• Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) pilot and scaling (p. 17).
• Embed digital tools into routine workflows (p. 18).
• Artificial Intelligence (AI) in clinical coding pilot (p. 17).
• Progress collaborative Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme (p. 17).
• Automation and Robotic Process Automation (RPA) introduction (p. 17).
• Zero-emission vehicles in salary sacrifice scheme (p. 27).
• Phase out paper records by summer 2027 (p. 28).
• Develop business cases from Heat Decarbonisation Plan (HDP) (p. 28).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
Comprehensive digital programme: Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) pilots, Artificial Intelligence (AI) in clinical coding (95% accuracy), single logon across Liverpool Adult Acute and Specialist Providers (LAASP), collaborative Electronic Patient Record (EPR) programme, automation, Robotic Process Automation (RPA), Patient Engagement Portal, digital letters, virtual appointments (p. 17-18, 28).
Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) pilots demonstrating improvements in clinical documentation time (p. 17).
Artificial Intelligence (AI) in clinical coding achieved 95% accuracy in proof-of-concept testing (p. 17).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Population health intelligence highlights inequalities in neurological outcomes linked to deprivation, access and late presentation (p. 5). Health Equity Plan to be delivered (p. 18). Anchor institution role recognised in addressing inequalities and delivering social value (p. 6, 19). Social value programme with £93m contribution in 2024/25 addresses wider determinants (p. 30).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: "Pioneering research and innovation" is a core strategic ambition (p. 6). Digital innovations: Ambient Voice Technology (AVT), Artificial Intelligence (AI) in clinical coding, Robotic Process Automation (RPA) (p. 17). Health Procurement Liverpool (HPL) shared service (p. 25). Spinal Community Assessment Day as new care model (p. 30-31). Sustainability innovations: elimination of desflurane and nitrous oxide, Heat Decarbonisation Plan (HDP) (p. 27-28).
OTHER: Health Procurement Liverpool (HPL) hosted by The Walton Centre, managing £200m non-pay expenditure across four partner sites (p. 25).
Social value programme delivered £93m contribution in 2024/25, using Themed Outcome Measures tool (p. 29-30).
Spinal Community Assessment Day reduced hospital attendances and demonstrated new care model (p. 30-31).
Elimination of desflurane and nitrous oxide reduced carbon emissions by over 222 tCO2e annually (p. 27).
Shared HR service across Liverpool Adult Acute and Specialist Providers (LAASP) to improve efficiency and value for money (p. 18-19).
University of Chester Medical School rotations from July 2027 to expand medical education (p. 18).
Neuro Lived Experience Group to co-design care pathways with patients and carers (p. 10, 12).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: "Sickness to prevention" is a core shift (p. 5, 8). Priorities include: earlier diagnosis, rapid specialist advice, expanded thrombectomy services, improved chronic neurological disease management to reduce long-term disability and avoidable admissions (p. 5). Quality Strategic Implementation Plan (QSIP) includes infection prevention and harm reduction (p. 9-11).
analysis.SUMMARY: 3.5m across North West and North Wales; unique national role. | Four ambitions  mapped to three national shifts. | Prevention and digital foundations strong; scale and productivity key. | Capital freedoms and productivity gains reinvested in digital and estates. | Thrombectomy expansion and chronic disease management prioritised. | AVT and AI coding pilots underway; collaborative EPR. | Specialist rehab expansion and Spinal Community Day. | Shared HR across LAASP; medical school rotations. | Population health intelligence and £93m social value. | HPL shared service; desflurane elimination; AVT pilots. | QIA process for all schemes; BAF reviewed.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) of £9.1m in 2026/27 (4.4%) (p. 21). Capital freedoms utilised for investment in digital and estates (p. 22). Social value contribution of £93m in 2024/25 demonstrates wider community benefit (p. 30). Shared services across Liverpool Adult Acute and Specialist Providers (LAASP) to reduce duplication (p. 18-20).
analysis.VISION: Our mission is excellence in neuroscience, to deliver meaningful patient outcomes, ground-breaking innovations, and exceptional teaching across our specialised disciplines. What unites us is a shared commitment to compassionate, life-changing care. (p.3)
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Workforce Strategy 2025-2028 with priorities: shared HR services across Liverpool Adult Acute and Specialist Providers (LAASP), expanded education and training (University of Chester Medical School rotations from July 2027), apprenticeship growth, leadership development, Health Equity Plan, wellbeing initiatives, refreshed values (Caring, Expert, Partner) (p. 7, 18-19). Investors in People Gold Award retained (p. 3).

Robotic surgical programme expansion to support cancer treatment and elective recovery (p. 25, 27).
Joint Chair and Chief Executive Officer (CEO) appointed November 2024, with single leadership team from November 2025 (p. 8-9).
Corporate staff transferred (TUPE) from WCHC to WUTH in December 2025, enabling single corporate functions from 2026/27 (p. 9).
Detailed pathway-level modelling used to test feasibility, sequencing, and benefits realisation for all service redesign (p. 75-78).
Integration of acute and community Trusts (WUTH and WCHC) is the central transformation vehicle, with merger targeted for Q1 2027/28 (p. 4, 7-9).
Wirral Provider Alliance (WPA) brings together NHS, Council, and Voluntary, Community, Faith and Social Enterprise (VCFSE) sector to coordinate place-based transformation (p. 49-53).
Five Drivers of the Deficit workstreams (Productivity, Integration, Urgent and Emergency Care (UEC), Sickness, Stabilising Services) structure the financial turnaround (p. 44-47).
Service Integration Principles (patient-focused, clinically led, benefits measurable) guide all pathway redesign, tested through detailed modelling (p. 75-78).
New Emergency Department opening mid-2026 to improve flow and reduce corridor care (p. 12, 23, 55).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Harm-free care priorities include falls, pressure ulcers, deconditioning (p. 28-29). Neighbourhood teams focus on frailty, dementia, end of life care (p. 20). Wirral Provider Alliance (WPA) programmes aligned to prevention shift (p. 50).
analysis.SUMMARY: Wirral's ageing population with stark east-west health divide. Serves c.400,000 | Six strategic objectives directly map to three national shifts. | Merger foundational to enable all shifts; digital foundations in place. | 1,242 WTE reduction and 20% corporate cut to fund integration. | Harm-free care priorities target frailty, falls, and deconditioning. | Single EPR by 2031; ambient voice and AI pilots underway | Wirral Provider Alliance drives neighbourhood model development. | Integration enables whole-pathway roles and sickness drive to 4.3%. | Core20PLUS5 focus in CYP; life expectancy gap drives neighbourhood targeting. | Acute-community merger as primary vehicle for system redesign. | Joint Board Assurance Frameworks, Integration Management Group, and annual cycle enable course correction.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: TRADE-OFFS: Workforce reduction of 1,242 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) over three years (p. 37-38) to deliver £96.5m Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) (p. 41, 43-44). Corporate overhead reduced by 20% through integration (p. 42). Resources shifted from acute to neighbourhood settings (p. 42).
analysis.VISION:
Shared vision mentioned throughout, but not made explicit. To deliver safe, high-quality and sustainable care through integration of acute and community services, aligned to the three shifts of the 10 Year Health Plan.
Joint Strategy to be published April 2026 (p. 17-18)
The plan's direction is set by the integration of WUTH and WCHC and is built around six core delivery areas (p. 20-32). The high-level goals are framed by the Trusts' joint work and the Wirral System Review, with the ambition to create a single, resilient organisation through merger by Q1 2027/28 (p. 4, 9).
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Joint People Strategy with integrated workforce planning across acute and community (p. 34-35). Role redesign, skill mix, digital upskilling, and apprenticeship pathways (p. 35-36). Sickness absence reduction programme targeting 4.3% (p. 39, 47). Leadership development, wellbeing, and retention strategies (p. 35-36, 39).

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan  aligned to 10 Year Health Plan three shifts: hospital to community, sickness to prevention, analogue to digital (p. 3, 5). Strategic priorities (Improve, Integrate, Innovate) mapped to these shifts (p. 5). Active partner in Healthier Wigan Partnership and Greater Manchester (GM) system (p. 3, 10-11).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Phased trajectory aims for financial breakeven by 2027/28, but 2026/27 plan is non-compliant (£8.2m deficit) (p. 18-19). Digital shift well-advanced with mature Electronic Patient Record (EPR) (p. 12, 38-41). Hospital to community shift progressing through Better Lives programme and neighbourhood work (p. 10-11, 21-22). Prevention shift supported by joint public health appointment and Anchor Partnership (p. 11).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Detailed risk tables for plan-wide risks (Years 1-5) and medium-term risks (Years 2-5) with mitigations (p. 24-28). Scenario planning and sensitivity analysis for financial risks (p. 18-19). Regular review of workforce plans and assumptions (p. 14-15). Five-year planning framework reviewed annually (p. 16).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Better Lives programme with Wigan partners focuses on supporting residents to live independently and transform urgent and emergency care (p. 10-11). Organisational redesign aligns acute and community services across pathways (p. 9). Development of integrated neighbourhood teams and community-based diagnostics (p. 21-22).
DELIVERY: • Planned deficit: £8.2m (non-compliant with national breakeven target) (p. 18-19).
• External cash support may be required (p. 19, 26).
• Block contract deconstruction impact assumed at 0.35% (£1.7m) (p. 19).
• Block contract deconstruction impact assumed at 0.5% (£2.5m) (p. 19).
• Steeper improvement trajectory required to meet 92% by 2028/29 (p. 12).
• Achieve 92% RTT target (implied by Medium-Term Planning Framework).
• Activity beyond 67% not funded by Integrated Care Board (ICB) (p. 12).
• Continued focus on flow, discharge, and community alternatives.
• Aim to meet national standards, though challenging.
• A&E target unlikely to be met due to trajectory and virtual ward funding reduction (p. 12).
• Cancer performance as a transformation priority (p. 8, 10, 12).
• Recovery plans implemented for colorectal and breast pathways (p. 7-8).
• Embed role redesign and productivity improvements.
• Further reduce agency and bank usage.
• Maintain adoption of core digital products (9 of 15 adopted) (p. 12, 38-41).
• Fully leverage digital for productivity and patient experience.
• Embed new divisional structure (Live Well & Urgent Care, Start Well & Planned Care) (p. 9).
• Mature left shift with reduced acute demand.
Fully integrated health and care system.
• Continue Better Lives programme with Wigan partners (p. 10-11).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Mature Electronic Patient Record (EPR) and strong digital foundations (p. 12, 23, 38-41). 9 of 15 core digital products adopted (p. 12, 38-41). Priorities: expand digital self-service, automation, real-time analytics, and interoperability (p. 20-21, 23). Federated Data Platform (FDP) deployment in progress (p. 40).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Joint public health appointment to target health inequalities (p. 11). Anchor Partnership addresses wider determinants of health (employment, education, housing) (p. 11). Health Inequalities and Prevention Plan developed following self-assessment (p. 22). Lived Experience Strategy includes inclusive community engagement (p. 31).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: Transformation priorities focus on Improvement, Integration, Innovation (p. 21). Digital self-service, automation, and real-time analytics (p. 20-21). Better Lives programme as locality-wide transformation model (p. 10-11). System Investment Fund with Wigan partners to accelerate change (p. 17).
OTHER: Joint Consultant in Public Health with Wigan Council embeds prevention in clinical pathways (p. 11).
Anchor Partnership leverages economic power to address wider determinants of health (employment, education, housing) (p. 11).
Better Lives programme demonstrates locality-wide transformation with Wigan partners (p. 10-11).
System Investment Fund with £100k initial contribution from each Wigan partner to accelerate change (p. 17).
Lived Experience Strategy with five strategic themes embeds patient voice in service design (p. 31).
5 Ds improvement approach (Define, Discover, Design, Deliver, Decide) provides consistent methodology (p. 10).
Figure 1 - Key health and wellbeing indicators for the Borough
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Joint appointment of a Consultant in Public Health with Wigan Council to embed prevention in clinical pathways (p. 11). Anchor Partnership addresses wider determinants of health (employment, education, housing) (p. 11). Prevention-focused roles in workforce planning (p. 13-14).
analysis.SUMMARY: Wigan Borough with high deprivation and ageing population. | Strategy mapped to three shifts via Improve, Integrate, Innovate. | Digital foundations strong, but financial non-compliance and system dependencies threaten pace. | Deficit support removed, requiring deep workforce reductions and risking cash sustainability. | Joint public health appointment and Anchor Partnership tackle wider determinants. | Mature EPR and nine core products adopted; FDP in progress. | Better Lives programme drives locality-based integrated care. | Role redesign aligned to three shifts with focus on wellbeing and productivity. | Anchor Partnership and Health Inequalities Plan target deprivation drivers. | System Investment Fund and Better Lives enable whole-system change. | Comprehensive risk tables with specific mitigations for years 1-5.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Deficit support funding (£8.9m) removed from 2026/27 (p. 18). Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) required at 5% (£28.8m) (p. 26). Workforce reductions needed to deliver savings (p. 26). External cash support may be required in 2026/27 (p. 19, 26). Block contract deconstruction will reduce income in later years (p. 19).
analysis.VISION: Our Strategy 2030 sets out our strategic framework based on the 4 Ps: Patients, People, Performance and Partnerships, and the 3 Is: Improve, Integrate, Innovate.	p. 5
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Workforce strategy aligned to three shifts: hospital to community (integrated roles), analogue to digital (digital skills), sickness to prevention (wellbeing models) (p. 13-14). Focus on role redesign, productivity, and reducing temporary staffing (p. 11-15). Leadership development and compassionate culture embedded (p. 11-15, 23).
